proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Multi-Modal, Contextual Reference Standard for Alzheimer's Disease Pathology: An Analogy from Oncology
proposals.motivation: Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) biomarkers, while central to Alzheimer's disease (AD) diagnosis, have limitations including invasiveness, sampling variability, and imperfect correlation with brain pathology. This mirrors the historical reliance on single-site tissue biopsies in oncology, which was superseded by multi-modal standards to account for tumor heterogeneity. Establishing a more robust, composite reference standard for AD is critical for improving diagnostic accuracy, clinical trial enrichment, and understanding disease complexity.
proposals.hypothesis: A composite reference standard, algorithmically integrating CSF Aβ42/40, p-tau, plasma biomarkers (e.g., p-tau217), tau-PET, and amyloid-PET, will provide a more accurate and representative classification of AD pathology than CSF biomarkers alone, particularly in diagnostically challenging cases.
proposals.method: We will use data from well-characterized cohorts (e.g., ADNI, BioFINDER) with multi-modal data. A supervised machine learning framework (e.g., random forest or gradient boosting) will be trained to classify participants, using neuropathological confirmation (when available) or expert consensus diagnosis based on all available longitudinal data as the target. The model will weight inputs from CSF, plasma, and PET modalities. Performance will be validated against clinical progression and compared to classifications based on CSF-only or PET-only criteria.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, algorithmically defined reference standard that better captures the spatial and biological heterogeneity of AD. It will directly inform revised diagnostic criteria and improve the selection of participants for clinical trials targeting specific pathological processes. Furthermore, it will establish a framework for integrating emerging fluid and imaging biomarkers.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel by explicitly adapting the oncology paradigm of a composite reference standard to AD neuropathology. It moves beyond comparing new biomarkers to a single CSF 'gold standard' and instead proposes a contextually integrated benchmark, a conceptual shift not yet fully realized in the AD field pre-2026.

proposals.title: Systematic Discrepancy Analysis: CSF Biomarkers, Synaptic Proteins, and Neuroimaging in the Alzheimer's Disease Continuum
proposals.motivation: Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) biomarkers for amyloid-β and tau are widely considered the gold standard for in vivo Alzheimer's disease (AD) pathology. However, this assumption is challenged by emerging pre-2026 evidence showing imperfect correlations between CSF measures, synaptic degeneration (a key pathophysiological step), and clinical symptoms. A critical, systematic evaluation of CSF's validity as a definitive reference is needed to refine diagnostic frameworks and therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CSF measures of amyloid-β and tau show significant, biologically meaningful discrepancies with other key pathological and functional readouts—specifically, CSF levels of pre-synaptic proteins (e.g., SNAP-25, synaptotagmin-1) and structural neuroimaging markers of neurodegeneration—particularly in the preclinical and prodromal stages of AD.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing longitudinal cohorts (e.g., ADNI, BioFINDER) of cognitively unimpaired, MCI, and mild AD dementia participants with paired CSF and MRI data. Using pre-2026 established assays, we will measure CSF Aβ42/40, p-tau181, and synaptic proteins (SNAP-25, GAP-43, synaptotagmin-1). We will employ latent class growth mixture modeling to identify subgroups with divergent longitudinal trajectories between amyloid/tau and synaptic/neurodegeneration markers. We will then use multivariate regression and mediation analyses to determine which biomarker combinations best predict subsequent cognitive decline, particularly memory scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for assessing when and why CSF amyloid/tau biomarkers fail to fully capture disease progression. It will directly inform clinical trial design by identifying subgroups where synaptic or neurodegenerative measures may be more relevant therapeutic targets or outcome measures than core CSF AD pathology.
proposals.novelty: While prior work correlates individual biomarkers, this proposal systematically tests the 'gold standard' premise by modeling multi-modal discrepancies and their clinical consequences, moving beyond simple correlation to a causal path model of divergent pathological axes.

proposals.title: A Synaptic-CSF Biomarker Panel for the Prediction of Clinical Alzheimer's Disease Diagnosis in Cognitively Impaired Individuals
proposals.motivation: Current Alzheimer's disease (AD) research and clinical trials heavily prioritize amyloid-beta (Aβ) pathology as the primary outcome, often equating biomarker positivity with disease. However, the clinical relevance of Aβ pathology in symptomatic individuals is variable, and synaptic dysfunction is a more direct correlate of cognitive impairment. A diagnostic framework that prioritizes the prediction of the clinical syndrome over pure pathology is urgently needed to align biomarker validation with patient-relevant outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: A panel of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) biomarkers, combining established amyloid/tau measures with novel synaptic proteins (e.g., neurogranin, SNAP-25, GAP-43), will outperform Aβ-centric models in predicting a clinical diagnosis of AD dementia in patients with mild cognitive impairment (MCI).
proposals.method: We will analyze pre-existing CSF samples and clinical data from a longitudinal cohort of MCI patients (e.g., ADNI, BioFINDER). Biomarkers will include Aβ42/40 ratio, p-tau, and synaptic proteins (neurogranin, SNAP-25). Using logistic regression and machine learning models (e.g., Random Forest or XGBoost), we will develop and cross-validate classifiers to predict progression to clinical AD dementia over 3-5 years. Model performance will be compared against a reference model based solely on the AT(N) framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, clinically oriented biomarker panel that directly addresses the symptomatic stage of AD. It will challenge the prevailing biomarker paradigm by demonstrating that a synaptic-protein-augmented model offers superior prognostic value for clinical diagnosis, thereby refining patient stratification for clinical trials and future diagnostic guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While synaptic biomarkers are known to correlate with cognition, their integration into a diagnostic model explicitly designed to predict the clinical syndrome—rather than to confirm amyloid pathology—represents a significant shift in validation strategy, moving from a pathology-first to a patient-first outcome.

proposals.title: Modeling the Required Analytical Performance of a Blood-Based p-tau217 Assay for Population-Level Screening of Alzheimer's Disease Pathology
proposals.motivation: Blood-based phosphorylated tau (p-tau217) is a highly promising biomarker for Alzheimer's disease (AD) pathology, with potential to revolutionize early detection. However, transitioning from research-grade assays to a scalable public health screening tool requires clear, evidence-based performance targets. Without defined thresholds for accuracy and precision, assay development and health system planning lack a critical benchmark for population-level implementation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deterministic simulation model, incorporating real-world pre-test probabilities and diagnostic pathways, can define the minimum analytical performance specifications (e.g., sensitivity, specificity, coefficient of variation) a blood-based p-tau217 assay must achieve to be cost-effective and clinically impactful in a national screening program.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-state Markov model simulating a screening cascade in a population with age-stratified AD risk. The model will incorporate pre-2026 data on p-tau217 distributions (from mass spectrometry and immunoassays), amyloid PET/Cerebrospinal Fluid (CSF) confirmatory test characteristics, and healthcare costs. Using Monte Carlo simulations, we will iteratively vary the input assay's diagnostic accuracy (ROC curves) and analytical precision (CV%) to determine the performance threshold where the screening program's cost per quality-adjusted life year (QALY) falls below a willingness-to-pay benchmark and where the number of unnecessary confirmatory tests is minimized.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, quantitative benchmark for the analytical performance required for population AD screening. It will directly guide diagnostic companies in developing fit-for-purpose assays and inform public health authorities on the feasibility and necessary infrastructure for large-scale implementation, thereby de-risking a critical transition in dementia care.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies compare assay performance in research cohorts, this project uniquely models the downstream public health outcomes to define a forward-looking performance standard, shifting the focus from pure analytical comparison to real-world system readiness.

proposals.title: Comparative Analytical Validation of Immunoassays Versus Mass Spectrometry for the Quantification of Cerebrospinal Fluid p-tau217 Ratio in Alzheimer's Disease Diagnostics
proposals.motivation: The p-tau217 ratio in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) is a highly promising biomarker for Alzheimer's disease (AD), with mass spectrometry (MS) considered the gold standard for its precise quantification. However, MS is complex and not widely accessible in clinical laboratories. Immunoassays offer a more practical, high-throughput alternative, but their analytical performance relative to MS for this specific ratio is not fully established, hindering clinical translation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that next-generation, high-sensitivity immunoassays can achieve analytical performance (precision, accuracy, and dynamic range) comparable to mass spectrometry for quantifying the CSF p-tau217 ratio, enabling reliable clinical application.
proposals.method: We will perform a head-to-head comparison using well-characterized CSF samples from the Gothenburg cohort, including individuals with AD, other dementias, and cognitively unimpaired controls. Samples will be analyzed in parallel using established liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) methods and at least two leading, commercially available, high-sensitivity immunoassay platforms (e.g., Simoa, Ella). We will assess key analytical parameters including intra- and inter-assay precision, limits of quantification, linearity, and correlation (Pearson/Spearman) between platforms. Diagnostic performance (AUC for discriminating AD) will be compared using receiver operating characteristic analysis.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a definitive, data-driven assessment of whether immunoassays are a viable alternative to MS for p-tau217 ratio measurement. It will inform clinical laboratories and biomarker consortia on the optimal platform for large-scale studies and future diagnostic implementation, directly addressing a key bottleneck in AD biomarker standardization.
proposals.novelty: While individual platforms have been validated against clinical outcomes, a rigorous, direct analytical comparison focusing specifically on the p-tau217 ratio—a metric more specific than absolute p-tau217 levels—between MS and multiple immunoassays is lacking. This study directly tests the prevailing assumption that MS is inherently superior for this application.

proposals.title: State-Space Estimation of Neuropathological Progression from Longitudinal Plasma Biomarkers in Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: Longitudinal plasma biomarkers like p-tau217 and Aβ42/40 ratio are revolutionizing Alzheimer's disease (AD) monitoring but are subject to measurement noise and biological variability. This complicates the precise staging of individuals and the prediction of future cognitive decline. A method to filter this noise and estimate the underlying, unobservable disease state is critically needed for personalized prognosis and trial enrichment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a state-space model, integrating known biomarker kinetics and sequential plasma measurements, can provide a more accurate and dynamic estimate of the true neuropathological burden and its rate of progression than raw biomarker values alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a state-space model where the hidden state represents the true neuropathological load (e.g., brain amyloid/tau). Using longitudinal plasma p-tau217 and Aβ42/40 data from established cohorts (e.g., BioFINDER, ADNI), we will apply a Kalman filter to optimally estimate this hidden state and its velocity at each time point. The model will incorporate prior knowledge on biomarker production/clearance rates and measurement error characteristics. Model outputs will be validated against gold-standard longitudinal amyloid-PET, tau-PET, and cognitive scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for dynamic disease staging, generating a filtered 'disease state' metric. It will improve the correlation between plasma biomarkers and concurrent neuropathology, enhance the prediction of near-term cognitive decline, and offer a probabilistic forecast of individual trajectories, aiding in clinical trial design and patient management.
proposals.novelty: While mixed-effects models are common, applying control theory state-space estimation (Kalman filtering) to model the hidden dynamics of AD pathology from blood biomarkers is a novel, engineering-inspired approach not yet explored in the AD biomarker field.

proposals.title: Direct Validation of Plasma p-tau217 Against Next-Generation Tau-PET: A Temporally Locked Cohort Study for Biomarker Calibration
proposals.motivation: Plasma phosphorylated tau (p-tau) biomarkers, such as p-tau217, show immense promise as accessible tools for Alzheimer's disease (AD) detection and staging. However, their precise relationship to the spatial distribution and burden of neurofibrillary tau tangles in the living brain remains inadequately characterized. Validating these liquid biopsies against the emerging gold standard of next-generation tau-PET imaging (e.g., using [18F]PI-2620 or [18F]MK-6240) is a critical, unmet step to establish their clinical utility and interpretability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that plasma p-tau217 levels, measured with single-molecule array (Simoa) or mass spectrometry-based assays, will show a strong, non-linear correlation with regional and global tau-PET signal, and that this calibrated relationship will accurately distinguish amyloid-positive individuals with high vs. low tau burden.
proposals.method: We will recruit 150 participants from memory clinics, spanning cognitively unimpaired, MCI, and AD dementia, all with confirmed amyloid status (CSF Aβ42/40 or amyloid-PET). Each participant will undergo plasma sampling and tau-PET imaging within a 48-hour window to minimize biological temporal variance. PET data will be quantified as standardized uptake value ratios (SUVRs) in Braak stage-defined regions of interest. The core analysis will employ non-linear regression and receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves to model the plasma-PET relationship and determine optimal plasma cut-points for predicting tau-PET positivity (SUVR >1.3). We will also assess associations with synaptic CSF biomarkers (e.g., neurogranin, GAP-43) as exploratory outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a definitive, point-by-point calibration of a leading plasma tau biomarker against in vivo tau pathology, establishing its dynamic range and clinical concordance. It will deliver validated plasma cut-points for tau staging, directly accelerating the development of blood-based diagnostic algorithms. Furthermore, by integrating synaptic markers, it will offer insights into the functional consequences of the plasma-predicted tau burden.
proposals.novelty: While plasma p-tau has been correlated with tau-PET in prior studies, the rigorous, temporally locked design—analogous to satellite ground-truthing—is novel and essential for precise calibration. Explicitly framing the validation challenge through this remote sensing analogy provides a clear methodological framework not previously applied in the AD biomarker field.

proposals.title: Quantifying Copathology Contributions to Clinical Heterogeneity in Alzheimer's Disease Using Interpretable Multi-Modal Machine Learning
proposals.motivation: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is frequently accompanied by non-AD copathologies (e.g., TDP-43, vascular disease, alpha-synuclein), which contribute to clinical heterogeneity and complicate prognosis and trial design. Current biomarker models, like the AT(N) framework, describe co-presence but cannot quantify the individual contribution of each pathology to a patient's specific symptom profile and disease stage. A method to disentangle these contributions is needed for biologically precise staging and future targeted therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model, trained on multi-modal data (CSF biomarkers, imaging, genetics, and clinical scores), can generate a personalized disease progression score and quantitatively attribute components of this score and observed clinical stage discordance to specific, measured copathologies.
proposals.method: We will develop an interpretable multi-task neural network. The model ingests baseline multi-modal data from cohorts like ADNI and BioFINDER. A shared representation layer feeds into: 1) a primary regression task predicting a continuous, validated disease progression score (e.g., CDR-SB), and 2) auxiliary prediction tasks for key copathology proxies (e.g., neurofilament light for axonal injury, specific MRI markers for vascular pathology). Using integrated gradients or layer-wise relevance propagation, we will decompose the primary risk score output into contributions attributable to each auxiliary pathology pathway, providing patient-specific attribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, interpretable computational tool that moves beyond correlative associations to provide quantitative, patient-specific estimates of copathology burden on clinical presentation. It will enable more precise prognostic stratification by explaining clinical heterogeneity and could guide the selection of patients for pathology-specific clinical trials.
proposals.novelty: While multi-modal ML models for staging exist, the explicit architectural design to decompose a continuous progression score into quantified contributions from known, measured copathologies represents a novel, oncology-inspired approach to etiological attribution in neurodegenerative diseases.

proposals.title: Development and Clinical Validation of a Point-of-Care Lateral Flow Assay for the Quantification of Plasma Phosphorylated Tau217 Ratio in Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: The plasma phosphorylated tau217 (p-tau217) biomarker has emerged pre-2026 as a highly specific indicator of Alzheimer's disease (AD) neuropathology, correlating strongly with amyloid-β PET status and cognitive decline. However, its measurement currently requires sophisticated, centralized laboratory equipment (e.g., mass spectrometry, Simoa), limiting accessibility for rapid screening in primary care, clinical trials, or resource-limited settings. A point-of-care (POC) test could revolutionize AD diagnostics by enabling immediate, actionable results.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that lateral flow assay (LFA) technology, adapted with high-affinity, phosphorylation-specific monoclonal antibody pairs, can be engineered to quantitatively measure the p-tau217 to total tau ratio in capillary blood with diagnostic accuracy comparable to established laboratory-based assays.
proposals.method: We will engineer a quantitative, multiplexed LFA using pre-2026, well-characterized monoclonal antibodies targeting the p-tau217 epitope and a non-phosphorylated tau epitope. The assay will employ fluorescent or gold nanoparticle labels for detection via a portable, calibrated reader. The method development will involve: 1) optimizing antibody conjugation and membrane conditions for sensitivity in the sub-pg/mL range; 2) establishing a robust internal calibration curve on the device; and 3) validating the assay's performance against gold-standard Simoa and mass spectrometry measurements in a well-characterized cohort, including cognitively unimpaired individuals, MCI, and AD dementia patients.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first proof-of-concept for a rapid, quantitative POC device for a core AD CSF/blood biomarker. It will establish the technical feasibility and initial clinical validity of LFA for p-tau217 ratio measurement, directly addressing the critical need for scalable, low-cost AD biomarker testing. Success would pave the way for large-scale screening studies and democratized access to AD pathology testing.
proposals.novelty: While LFAs are established for infectious diseases, their application to quantitatively measure subtle, disease-specific protein phosphorylation ratios in neurodegeneration is novel. This proposal innovatively merges Kaj Blennow's expertise in synaptic/AD CSF protein quantification with POC device engineering, a significant methodological leap from his prior work on lab-based biomarker associations.

proposals.title: A Subject-Specific Vulnerability-Prior Bayesian Model for Estimating Regional Amyloid-β and Tau Pathology from Plasma Biomarkers in Preclinical Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: Plasma biomarkers for amyloid-β (Aβ) and tau are promising for Alzheimer's disease (AD) screening, but they lack regional specificity, which is critical for understanding early pathology spread and clinical progression. Current models that map plasma levels to brain-wide pathology ignore substantial inter-individual heterogeneity in vulnerability due to genetics, vascular health, and brain structure. A method to estimate subject-specific regional pathology from accessible biomarkers would significantly enhance preclinical staging and personalized risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a personalized neurobiological vulnerability prior, derived from baseline MRI, vascular markers (e.g., blood pressure, APOE genotype), and polygenic risk scores, into a Bayesian inverse model will yield more accurate and biologically plausible estimates of regional Aβ and tau pathology from plasma biomarker levels than models using plasma data alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian model. The likelihood function will relate regional pathology (latent variables) to observed plasma Aβ42/40, p-tau181, and p-tau217 levels, using established kinetic relationships. The key innovation is the subject-specific prior, built via a regression model trained on pre-2026 ADNI data, which predicts baseline regional vulnerability from structural MRI (cortical thickness in default mode network), vascular health indicators, and genetic risk. Inference will be performed using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (Stan) to generate posterior distributions of pathology for each brain region per individual.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for non-invasive, regionally specific pathology estimation, directly addressing a major limitation of plasma biomarkers. It will advance the field by enabling more precise subject-level mapping in preclinical cohorts, which can refine enrollment for clinical trials and improve understanding of individual progression pathways. The model will also formally quantify how modifiable (vascular) and non-modifiable (genetic) risk factors shape pathology deposition.
proposals.novelty: While others have linked plasma biomarkers to global PET burden, this model uniquely fuses multi-modal baseline data (MRI, genetics, vascular health) into a formal Bayesian prior to constrain the ill-posed inverse problem of estimating regional pathology from systemic measures, explicitly accounting for individual vulnerability.

proposals.title: Inverse Modeling of Plasma Amyloid-β and Tau to Estimate Regional Brain Pathology in Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: Plasma biomarkers for Alzheimer's disease (AD), such as Aβ42/40 and p-tau, provide accessible systemic measures but lack spatial information. Neuroimaging (PET) offers regional pathology mapping but is costly and not widely available. A method to infer regional brain amyloid and tau burden from a blood sample would bridge this gap, enabling more precise disease staging and monitoring in clinical and research settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an individual's plasma biomarker profile is a weighted sum of contributions from distinct brain regions, and that by solving this inverse problem using co-registered imaging and fluid data, we can generate spatially resolved estimates of pathology from systemic measures alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a biophysical forward model describing biomarker flux from brain tissue to blood, parameterized by regional cerebral blood flow and blood-brain barrier permeability from structural and arterial spin labeling MRI. Using a dataset of paired plasma Aβ/p-tau assays and amyloid/tau-PET scans, we will treat PET-defined regional SUVRs as the ground truth 'source' distribution. We will apply constrained linear inversion and Bayesian inference frameworks (e.g., using Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampling) to solve for the regional weights that best predict the observed plasma levels, validating the model in a held-out cohort.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel computational framework to translate a single plasma biomarker measurement into a probabilistic map of likely regional pathology. It will provide a new tool for non-invasive disease mapping, potentially identifying subtypes based on spatial patterns and enriching clinical trials by predicting spatial progression from baseline blood draws.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in directly adapting geophysical inverse problem-solving techniques (seismic tomography) to neurobiology. While prior work correlates global plasma and PET measures, this method explicitly models the brain as a spatially continuous source medium to perform a formal deconvolution of the systemic signal.

proposals.title: Leveraging CSF Amyloid-β42 and Synaptic Biomarker Profiles to Triage Patients for Non-Alzheimer's Disease Dementia Workup: A Diagnostic Utility Study
proposals.motivation: In the pre-2026 diagnostic landscape, CSF amyloid-β42 (Aβ42) is a core biomarker for Alzheimer's disease (AD) pathology, with a high negative predictive value (NPV) for ruling out AD. However, patients with cognitive impairment but normal Aβ42 levels present a diagnostic challenge, as they may have diverse non-AD etiologies (e.g., LBD, FTD, vascular). Efficiently identifying and directing these patients towards appropriate, targeted diagnostic evaluations for non-AD dementias is a critical unmet clinical need to avoid misdiagnosis and enable proper management.
proposals.hypothesis: A dual-biomarker profile combining normal CSF Aβ42 (ruling out AD) with abnormal levels of pre-synaptic (e.g., neurogranin, SNAP-25) or other non-AD associated proteins will efficiently identify patients with a high probability of having a non-AD dementia, thereby streamlining subsequent diagnostic workup.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing, well-characterized cohorts (e.g., ADNI, BioFINDER) of patients with mild cognitive impairment or dementia who have undergone comprehensive CSF biomarker profiling and final clinical diagnosis. Using pre-specified cut-offs for CSF Aβ42 (normal) and established ELISA/SIMOA assays for synaptic proteins (neurogranin, SNAP-25, GAP-43) and neurodegeneration markers (e.g., NfL), we will calculate the NPV, positive predictive value (PPV), and likelihood ratios of different biomarker profiles for predicting a non-AD etiology. Statistical analyses will employ logistic regression models to assess associations, adjusting for age, sex, and APOE ε4 status.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a concrete, biomarker-based algorithm to triage patients with cognitive impairment away from the AD diagnostic pathway and towards targeted investigations for other dementias. It will quantify the diagnostic utility of synaptic biomarkers in the context of normal Aβ42, adding a practical, translational dimension to their established role as markers of synaptic integrity. The findings could directly inform clinical guidelines for the efficient use of biomarker testing in memory clinics.
proposals.novelty: While synaptic biomarkers are studied for their association with AD progression, their specific utility in a triage strategy—using their combination with normal Aβ42 to positively flag non-AD cases—is a novel application. This shifts the focus from confirming AD to actively ruling it out and prompting investigation of alternative causes, a perspective not fully explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Modeling Peripheral Clearance to Decouple Plasma Amyloid-β and Synaptic Protein Levels from Central Pathology in Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: Plasma biomarkers like amyloid-β (Aβ) and synaptic proteins (e.g., neurogranin, GAP-43) are promising for Alzheimer's disease (AD) diagnosis and monitoring, but their levels show variable correlations with central CSF measures and neuroimaging. This variability may stem from individual differences in peripheral clearance mechanisms, such as liver metabolism, renal function, and blood-brain barrier efficiency, which could decouple plasma concentrations from brain pathology, limiting their interpretability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that inter-individual variability in peripheral clearance organ function and systemic physiology accounts for a significant portion of the discordance between plasma and CSF/neuroimaging biomarkers of Aβ and synaptic integrity, independent of brain pathology burden.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing longitudinal cohort data (e.g., from ADNI or BioFINDER) with paired plasma/CSF biomarkers (Aβ42/40, p-tau181, neurogranin), neuroimaging (amyloid-PET), and clinical data. Using structural equation modeling (SEM), we will construct a latent variable model where plasma biomarker levels are regressed on: 1) a latent 'brain pathology' factor (defined by CSF biomarkers and amyloid-PET), and 2) direct measured peripheral factors (eGFR for renal function, liver enzyme panels, vascular markers, age, BMI). The model will quantify the variance in plasma levels explained by peripheral factors versus central pathology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for understanding the biological confounders of plasma AD biomarkers, moving beyond simple correlation studies. It will inform the development of adjusted plasma biomarker algorithms that more accurately reflect brain-specific changes, enhancing their utility in clinical trials and diagnostics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work establishes associations, this study explicitly models peripheral clearance as a latent decoupling mechanism using causal inference frameworks (SEM), directly testing a systemic physiological hypothesis for biomarker discordance rather than treating it as noise.

proposals.title: Longitudinal trajectories of amyloid, tau, and synaptic CSF biomarkers in ethnically diverse older adults with subjective cognitive decline
proposals.motivation: Most longitudinal biomarker studies in Alzheimer's disease (AD) have focused on younger-old, predominantly non-Hispanic White cohorts, limiting generalizability. It is unknown whether the temporal sequence of amyloid-β (Aβ) aggregation, tau pathology, and synaptic dysfunction, as measured in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), differs in older age groups (e.g., 80+) or across ethnicities, which is critical for accurate prognosis and trial design in diverse populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in older (≥80 years) and ethnically diverse (including Black and Hispanic) individuals with subjective cognitive decline, the trajectories of CSF synaptic biomarkers (e.g., neurogranin, SNAP-25) will show a steeper decline relative to Aβ and tau pathology, and that these biomarker dynamics will be more strongly associated with memory scores than in younger, less diverse cohorts.
proposals.method: We will longitudinally analyze CSF samples from a multi-ethnic cohort (n=400, aged 70-95, 40% non-White) with baseline subjective cognitive decline, collected over 5 years. Using established ELISAs and mass spectrometry (pre-2026 methods), we will measure Aβ42/40 ratio, p-tau181, neurogranin, and SNAP-25 at three timepoints. Biomarker trajectories will be modeled using linear mixed-effects models, with age, ethnicity, and APOE ε4 status as covariates. Associations with longitudinal memory scores will be tested using multivariate regression.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal data on the co-evolution of core AD and synaptic CSF biomarkers in an older, ethnically diverse at-risk population. It will clarify whether synaptic biomarker trajectories are a more sensitive marker of early cognitive decline in advanced age and across ethnic groups, informing more inclusive biological models of AD progression.
proposals.novelty: While Blennow's prior work linked synaptic protein levels to memory in cross-sectional, non-dementia cohorts, this proposal uniquely applies longitudinal tracking specifically to understudied older and ethnically diverse groups, testing if the established biomarker cascade model holds in these populations.

proposals.title: Modeling the optimal sampling frequency of plasma p-tau and synaptic biomarkers for cost-effective clinical monitoring in at-risk individuals
proposals.motivation: Plasma biomarkers like p-tau217 and synaptic proteins (e.g., neurogranin, GAP-43) are promising for tracking Alzheimer's disease progression. However, the optimal frequency for repeated sampling to detect meaningful change while minimizing cost and patient burden is unknown. Determining this is critical for designing feasible and scalable clinical monitoring protocols for at-risk populations, such as those with subjective cognitive decline or amyloid positivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a data-driven model can identify a non-linear, stage-dependent relationship between biomarker change and time, enabling the calculation of cost-minimized sampling intervals that maintain high sensitivity for detecting clinically relevant progression.
proposals.method: We will use longitudinal data from established cohorts (e.g., ADNI, BioFINDER) with serial plasma p-tau217 and synaptic protein measurements. We will fit non-linear mixed-effects models to characterize individual biomarker trajectories. Using these models in a simulation framework, we will apply Bayesian optimization and cost-effectiveness analysis to evaluate different sampling schedules. The objective function will balance the cost of sampling against the penalty for delayed detection of a pre-defined critical biomarker change point.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework for designing plasma biomarker monitoring schedules, directly informing clinical trial enrichment and future preventative care pathways. It will establish whether synaptic biomarkers, reflecting downstream pathology, require different sampling frequencies than upstream amyloid/tau markers for optimal monitoring.
proposals.novelty: While previous work establishes associations between single-timepoint biomarker levels and outcomes, this proposal innovatively applies dynamical systems modeling and operations research techniques to the temporal scheduling problem in biomarker science, moving from association to optimized application.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a High-Throughput, Cost-Effective Plasma p-tau217 Immunoassay for Population-Level Cognitive Screening in Primary Care
proposals.motivation: Plasma phosphorylated tau217 (p-tau217) has emerged pre-2026 as a highly specific biomarker for Alzheimer's disease (AD) pathology, strongly correlating with amyloid-β PET and tau PET. However, its translation into clinical practice is hindered by the need for specialized, low-throughput mass spectrometry assays, which are expensive and unsuitable for large-scale screening. A scalable, automated immunoassay is urgently needed to enable early detection in primary care, where most cognitive complaints are first presented.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel, high-throughput, single-molecule array (Simoa) immunoassay for plasma p-tau217 can be developed and validated to show equivalent diagnostic accuracy for amyloid-β positivity as established mass spectrometry methods, but at a fraction of the cost and time, making population-level screening feasible.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel Simoa assay using a pair of high-affinity, monoclonal anti-tau antibodies targeting the p-tau217 epitope. The assay will be optimized for a fully automated HD-X Analyzer platform. Analytical validation will include precision, linearity, and limit of detection. Clinical validation will be performed in a well-characterized cohort (e.g., the Swedish BioFINDER study), comparing the Simoa p-tau217 levels to established CSF/p-tau217 mass spectrometry, amyloid PET status, and cognitive scores (MMSE), using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis and multivariate regression models controlling for age and APOE ε4 status.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, fully automated high-throughput workflow for plasma p-tau217 measurement. It will directly address the critical bottleneck of scalability, offering a practical tool for primary care physicians to triage patients for specialist referral or advanced diagnostics. The project will generate a clear cost-benefit analysis model for population-level screening programs.
proposals.novelty: While plasma p-tau217 is known to be a strong biomarker, all pre-2026 quantification methods rely on resource-intensive mass spectrometry. This proposal is novel in its specific focus on engineering a disruptive, scalable immunoassay platform tailored for the high-volume, low-cost demands of primary care, rather than specialized memory clinics.

proposals.title: Synaptic and Amyloid Biomarker Profiles for Tracking Target Engagement of the p75NTR Modulator LM11A-31 in Early Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: LM11A-31, a small molecule p75NTR modulator, has shown neuroprotective and amyloid-reducing effects in preclinical models and is entering early-phase clinical trials. A critical gap is the lack of validated, sensitive biomarkers to demonstrate its biological effect in the human brain, which is essential for efficient trial design and proof-of-concept. Pre-2026 work by Blennow and others has established that CSF synaptic proteins (e.g., neurogranin, SNAP-25, GAP-43) and amyloid PET are key markers of Alzheimer's pathology and synaptic integrity, but their specific utility for monitoring p75NTR modulation is unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite biomarker signature combining CSF measures of pre-synaptic (SNAP-25) and post-synaptic (neurogranin) proteins, along with amyloid PET, will be the most sensitive and specific for detecting the biological effects of LM11A-31, reflecting its dual action on synaptic resilience and amyloid pathology.
proposals.method: We will analyze baseline and longitudinal CSF and neuroimaging data from a Phase II trial of LM11A-31 in early Alzheimer's disease. CSF levels of synaptic proteins (neurogranin, SNAP-25, GAP-43, synaptotagmin-1) and core AD biomarkers (Aβ42/40, p-tau, t-tau) will be measured using validated immunoassays. Amyloid PET (e.g., [18F]flutemetamol) will quantify cortical amyloid burden. We will employ multivariate linear mixed-effects models to identify biomarkers showing significant treatment-by-time interaction. Machine learning (LASSO regression) will be used to refine a parsimonious composite biomarker score predictive of clinical progression and treatment response.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first validated biomarker panel for tracking target engagement of a p75NTR modulator in humans, directly informing go/no-go decisions for later-phase trials. It will advance the field of precision neurotherapeutics by linking a specific neuroprotective mechanism (p75NTR modulation) to quantifiable in vivo biological changes, moving beyond generic amyloid and tau measures.
proposals.novelty: While synaptic biomarkers are associated with cognitive decline, their specific application for monitoring a p75NTR-targeted therapy is novel. The integration of multi-modal CSF synaptic markers with amyloid PET to create a mechanism-specific composite score represents a significant advance over analyzing single biomarkers in isolation.

proposals.title: Spatial mapping of GAP-43 mRNA expression and tau pathology across connected brain regions in Alzheimer's disease
proposals.motivation: Synaptic dysfunction is a critical early event in Alzheimer's disease (AD), preceding neuronal loss. Growth-associated protein 43 (GAP-43) is a key presynaptic protein involved in plasticity, and its CSF levels correlate with memory in non-demented individuals. However, the regional transcriptional regulation of GAP-43 in relation to the spread of tau pathology, a major driver of cognitive decline, remains unknown, limiting our understanding of synaptic vulnerability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatially defined reductions in GAP-43 mRNA expression in specific cortical layers and subregions are tightly coupled to the local burden of hyperphosphorylated tau pathology, and that this relationship follows the known anatomical connectivity patterns of early tau spread in AD.
proposals.method: We will apply the 10x Genomics Visium spatial transcriptomics platform to post-mortem human brain tissue from the entorhinal cortex, hippocampus, and inferior temporal gyrus of AD cases and controls. Consecutive sections will be stained for hyperphosphorylated tau (AT8 antibody) for precise co-registration. We will quantify GAP-43 mRNA expression in spatially defined regions of interest (ROIs) using the Space Ranger and Seurat pipelines. Statistical associations between local GAP-43 expression and tau burden will be assessed using spatial generalized linear models, and connectivity-based patterns will be analyzed using graph theory approaches.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first spatially resolved transcriptomic map of a key synaptic plasticity gene in relation to tau pathology in AD. It will identify specific cortical layers and circuits where synaptic transcriptional responses are most vulnerable, offering novel mechanistic insights into disease progression. The findings could reveal new regional biomarkers for synaptic integrity beyond CSF measures.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Blennow and others has established CSF GAP-43 as a synaptic biomarker, this study moves to the tissue level with spatial resolution, directly testing the local relationship between gene expression and pathology in connected regions, which has not been done.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Cerebrospinal Fluid Profiling of GAP-43 and Phospho-Tau in an APP/PS1 Mouse Model Reveals a Temporal Sequence of Synaptic Dysfunction and Neurodegeneration
proposals.motivation: In Alzheimer's disease (AD), the temporal relationship between synaptic pathology, marked by proteins like GAP-43, and neuronal injury, marked by tau, is poorly defined in the preclinical phase. This gap hinders the understanding of disease progression and the development of targeted, stage-specific biomarkers. Translating recent human CSF findings on synaptic proteins to a controlled, longitudinal animal model is critical to establish causality and sequence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a transgenic mouse model of amyloidosis, CSF levels of the presynaptic protein GAP-43 will increase significantly earlier than phospho-tau species, reflecting an initial phase of synaptic dysfunction and compensatory plasticity that precedes overt neurodegeneration.
proposals.method: We will use APPswe/PS1dE9 mice and wild-type littermates. Serial, low-volume CSF sampling will be performed via cisterna magna puncture at 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 months of age (n=15/group). CSF levels of GAP-43 and phospho-tau (e.g., p-tau181, p-tau217) will be quantified using established, pre-2026 ultrasensitive immunoassays (Simoa or MSD). Amyloid-β levels will be measured in parallel. Temporal sequences will be analyzed using mixed-effects models and cross-lagged panel analysis to infer directional relationships between biomarker trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution, longitudinal map of synaptic and neuronal injury biomarkers in a preclinical AD model, directly testing the sequence implied by cross-sectional human studies. It will establish whether GAP-43 is a leading indicator of synaptic stress in amyloidosis and clarify its position relative to tau pathology. The findings will inform the interpretation of human biomarker studies and guide the selection of outcome measures for preclinical trials targeting synaptic resilience.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has measured these biomarkers at endpoint, the novel application of frequent longitudinal CSF sampling in mice to define the dynamic, causal sequence of GAP-43 and tau changes represents a significant methodological and conceptual advance over static snapshots.

proposals.title: Intra-individual variability of serum inflammatory cytokines: implications for single-timepoint assessment of chronic neuroinflammation in Alzheimer's disease
proposals.motivation: In Alzheimer's disease research, serum cytokine levels are increasingly used as accessible biomarkers of chronic neuroinflammation, a key pathological driver. However, this approach critically assumes that a single blood draw accurately reflects a stable, long-term inflammatory state, an assumption largely untested. If cytokine levels exhibit significant biological variability over time, single measurements could misclassify patients and confound associations with amyloid pathology and synaptic dysfunction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that key serum cytokines proposed as neuroinflammatory biomarkers (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1β, YKL-40) show substantial intra-individual biological variability over weeks to months in older adults, rendering a single timepoint measurement an unreliable index of chronic neuroinflammation.
proposals.method: We will leverage longitudinal serum samples from the Gothenburg H70 Birth Cohort studies, selecting 60 cognitively unimpaired older adults with archived samples at 0, 1, 3, and 6 months. Using validated multiplex immunoassays (e.g., Meso Scale Discovery), we will quantify a panel of 15 cytokines. Intra-individual variability will be assessed using the intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) and coefficient of variation (CV). We will further analyze associations between cytokine variability and established CSF biomarkers of amyloid-β (Aβ42/40), tau, and synaptic proteins (e.g., neurogranin, GAP-43) measured at baseline, using linear mixed-effects models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first rigorous, longitudinal data on the temporal stability of serum cytokines in an aging population, directly testing a foundational assumption in blood-based biomarker research. It will establish reference ICCs to inform future study design and determine if variability itself is linked to core AD pathology, potentially refining the interpretation of inflammatory markers in relation to synaptic and cognitive outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has measured cytokines cross-sectionally, this study directly challenges the methodological premise of single-timepoint sampling by quantifying biological variability, integrating this with core AD biomarkers in a pre-dementia cohort to contextualize the findings within known disease pathways.

proposals.title: Cerebrospinal fluid synaptic and inflammatory protein signatures across neurodegenerative and psychiatric disorders: a comparative correlative analysis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Blennow's work, has established that CSF levels of synaptic proteins (e.g., neurogranin, SNAP-25) correlate with cognitive function in Alzheimer's disease and preclinical stages. However, it is unclear if these synaptic biomarkers reflect a common pathway of neural circuit disruption or are disease-specific. Applying this established correlative framework to other conditions with known neuroinflammatory components, like ALS and major depressive disorder (MDD), could disentangle general markers of synaptic injury from those unique to specific pathologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a core CSF signature of synaptic dysfunction, characterized by elevated neurogranin and SNAP-25, will be common across Alzheimer's disease, ALS, and MDD and correlate with cognitive/motor symptom severity, while distinct inflammatory protein profiles (e.g., specific cytokines, GFAP) will differentiate the disorders.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing and prospectively collected CSF samples from well-characterized cohorts: Alzheimer's disease (cognitively normal to dementia), ALS, and MDD patients, alongside healthy controls. Using multiplex immunoassays (e.g., Meso Scale Discovery), we will quantify a panel of pre-specified synaptic (neurogranin, SNAP-25, GAP-43) and neuroinflammatory (GFAP, YKL-40, IL-6, TNF-α) proteins. Correlations between protein levels and domain-specific clinical scores (memory, motor, mood) will be assessed using partial least squares correlation (PLSC) analysis, controlling for age and sex, to identify latent multivariate patterns. Disease-specific signatures will be identified via supervised machine learning (e.g., random forest classification).
proposals.contribution: This study will determine the generalizability of synaptic CSF biomarkers beyond Alzheimer's disease, potentially identifying a transdiagnostic marker of synaptic integrity. It will also delineate condition-specific inflammatory signatures, providing a more precise biological stratification of these disorders for future therapeutic targeting and clinical trial enrichment.
proposals.novelty: While synaptic CSF markers are established in Alzheimer's, their systematic comparison across etiologically distinct conditions like ALS and MDD is novel. The integrated analysis of synaptic and inflammatory proteins within a single correlative framework to find shared and unique pathways represents a significant advance over single-disease studies.

proposals.title: Tau as a Consequence of Synaptic Dysfunction: A Causal Mediation Analysis in Men with Preclinical Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the dominant Alzheimer's disease (AD) model posits that tau pathology is a primary driver of cognitive decline. However, epidemiological studies, including work by Blennow's group, show stronger associations between tau biomarkers and cognitive outcomes in men, suggesting a sex-specific relationship. This raises the critical question of whether tau is a direct cause or a downstream consequence of a more proximal pathological process, such as synaptic failure, particularly in men where the association is strongest.
proposals.hypothesis: In cognitively unimpaired men with amyloid-β (Aβ) pathology, elevated CSF tau (p-tau) is not a direct cause of memory decline but is instead a consequence of synaptic dysfunction, which itself is the primary mediator between Aβ aggregation and early cognitive symptoms.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal data from the Swedish BioFINDER-2 cohort (pre-2026), focusing on Aβ-positive men without dementia. Using established pre-2026 immunoassays, we will measure baseline CSF levels of core AD biomarkers (Aβ42/40, p-tau), presynaptic (e.g., neurogranin, SNAP-25) and postsynaptic (e.g., GAP-43) proteins. We will apply causal mediation analysis (using the R package 'mediation') to statistically test if the effect of baseline Aβ status on 2-year memory decline is mediated sequentially: first through synaptic protein levels, and then through subsequent changes in p-tau levels, rather than through a direct Aβ→tau→cognition pathway.
proposals.contribution: This work will challenge the linear causal model of AD by providing evidence for a sex-specific, sequential pathogenic cascade where synaptic failure precedes and drives tau pathology. It will directly inform therapeutic strategies, suggesting that interventions preserving synaptic integrity in preclinical men may be more effective than those targeting tau alone. The findings will also refine biomarker interpretation, positioning synaptic CSF proteins as more proximal indicators of early decline in men.
proposals.novelty: While synaptic biomarkers are known to correlate with cognition, this study innovatively positions them as upstream mediators of tau pathology in a specific at-risk population (Aβ+ men), formally testing an inverted causal sequence using robust pre-2026 statistical methods for mediation.

proposals.title: Sex-stratified models of cognitive decline: Integrating synaptic, amyloid, and neurodegenerative CSF biomarkers for improved diagnostic and prognostic accuracy in Alzheimer's disease
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research consistently shows sex differences in Alzheimer's disease (AD) prevalence, progression, and biomarker profiles. However, clinical diagnostic and prognostic models are largely unisex, potentially reducing their accuracy for individual patients. This is critical as the field moves towards biologically targeted therapies, where precise staging and prediction are essential for clinical trial enrollment and personalized management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sex-specific risk prediction algorithms, which differentially weight the relationships between core AD CSF biomarkers (amyloid-β, tau, neurodegeneration) and synaptic CSF biomarkers (e.g., neurogranin, SNAP-25, GAP-43) with cognitive outcomes, will provide superior diagnostic and prognostic accuracy compared to unisex models in individuals across the AD continuum.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal data from established cohorts (e.g., ADNI, BioFINDER) of cognitively unimpaired, MCI, and AD dementia participants with CSF measures and cognitive scores. Sex-stratified multivariate linear mixed-effects models and Cox proportional hazards models will be used to define biomarker-cognition relationships. These relationships will inform the development of sex-specific diagnostic (e.g., penalized logistic regression) and prognostic (e.g., random survival forests) algorithms. Model performance will be validated in independent cohorts using metrics like AUC and C-index.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first validated, sex-stratified diagnostic and prognostic models that integrate synaptic integrity with the AT(N) framework. It will provide a concrete tool for more accurate patient stratification in research and clinical settings. Furthermore, it will elucidate the distinct biological pathways driving cognitive decline in men and women, informing future therapeutic development.
proposals.novelty: While sex differences in single biomarkers are known, creating formal, optimized prediction algorithms separately for men and women is a novel translational step. The explicit integration of pre-2026 findings on synaptic CSF protein levels (Blennow et al.) with core AD pathology to build these sex-specific models is a unique and mechanistically informed approach.

proposals.title: Experimental Manipulation of Sex Hormones in Cellular and Rodent Models to Elucidate Effects on Tau Pathology and Astrocytic GFAP Expression
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 epidemiological and clinical biomarker studies, including work by Dr. Blennow, consistently show sex differences in Alzheimer's disease (AD) prevalence and biomarker profiles, with women showing greater tau burden and faster cognitive decline post-menopause. However, the causal role of sex hormones like estradiol and testosterone in driving AD-related proteinopathies, specifically tau hyperphosphorylation and glial activation, remains mechanistically unclear. This gap hinders the development of targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that depletion of sex hormones (e.g., via gonadectomy or pharmacological blockade) will increase tau phosphorylation and GFAP expression in relevant models, and that hormone replacement will mitigate these effects, with the response being cell-type and tau isoform specific.
proposals.method: We will use two complementary models: 1) Human iPSC-derived neurons (including isogenic lines with MAPT mutations) and astrocytes, treated with hormone-depleted media and supplemented with 17β-estradiol or dihydrotestosterone, measuring phospho-tau (e.g., p-tau181, p-tau217) and GFAP via SIMOA and Western blot. 2) Tauopathy mouse models (e.g., P301S) subjected to gonadectomy with/without hormone replacement. Brain tissue will be analyzed for tau pathology (AT8 immunohistochemistry) and GFAP levels, with synaptic markers (e.g., neurogranin, SNAP-25) assessed in CSF/brain homogenate to link to Dr. Blennow's synaptic protein expertise.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide direct experimental evidence for sex hormones as modulators of core AD proteinopathies, moving beyond associative clinical data. It will identify whether tau and GFAP are primary targets of hormone action, informing the biological rationale for hormone-based therapies. The data will also help interpret sex-specific patterns in clinical CSF biomarkers of tau and glial activation.
proposals.novelty: While observational studies link sex to AD risk, few pre-2026 studies experimentally manipulate hormone levels in conjunction with state-of-the-art tau and GFAP quantification in controlled models. Combining iPSC models of human neurons with in vivo tauopathy models to dissect cell-type-specific effects on both tau and glial responses is a novel, mechanistic approach.

proposals.title: Synaptic and Inflammatory CSF Profiles Underlying Cognitive Resilience in Women with High Tau Pathology
proposals.motivation: A significant proportion of individuals with high Alzheimer's disease (AD) pathology, measured by CSF biomarkers like amyloid-β and tau, do not exhibit the expected rate of cognitive decline, a phenomenon termed cognitive resilience. Understanding the biological basis of this resilience is crucial for identifying protective mechanisms and therapeutic targets. Women are at higher risk for AD, yet some show remarkable resilience, making them a critical population for study to uncover sex-specific protective factors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that women with high CSF p-tau levels but preserved cognition will exhibit distinct CSF proteomic profiles, characterized by higher levels of synaptic integrity proteins (e.g., neurogranin, SNAP-25, GAP-43) and a more favorable inflammatory milieu (e.g., lower pro-inflammatory cytokines), compared to cognitively declining women with similarly high tau pathology.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing longitudinal cohort data (e.g., ADNI, BioFINDER) to identify resilient (high p-tau, slow decline on longitudinal cognitive scores) and non-resilient (high p-tau, fast decline) female participants. Using pre-2026 established platforms (e.g., Olink, Luminex), we will perform targeted multiplex immunoassays on baseline CSF to quantify panels of pre-synaptic, post-synaptic, and inflammatory proteins. Statistical analyses will employ multivariate logistic regression models, adjusting for age and APOE ε4 status, to identify protein signatures associated with resilience. Machine learning approaches (e.g., Random Forest or LASSO regression) will be used to build a predictive model of resilience from the proteomic data.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly identify specific CSF protein signatures, particularly synaptic and inflammatory markers, that are associated with cognitive resilience in the context of high tau pathology in women. It will provide novel insights into the biological mechanisms that buffer against tau-induced neurodegeneration. The findings could lead to new biomarkers for resilience and inform the development of therapies aimed at enhancing these protective pathways.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Blennow and others has linked synaptic CSF protein levels to memory, this proposal uniquely focuses on defining a protective signature specifically in a high-risk, high-pathology group (women with high tau), moving beyond mere association to directly address the 'dual problem' of resilience. The integration of synaptic and inflammatory profiles within a resilience framework is a novel, systems-level approach.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Standardized Protocol for Disclosing Alzheimer's Disease Biomarker Status to Cognitively Normal Individuals
proposals.motivation: The clinical use of Alzheimer's disease (AD) biomarkers, such as CSF amyloid-β and synaptic proteins, is expanding into cognitively normal populations for risk stratification. However, pre-2026, there are no standardized, evidence-based guidelines for communicating these complex results, posing risks of psychological harm, misunderstanding, and inappropriate clinical action. This gap is critical to address as biomarker disclosure moves from research to clinical practice.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, multi-component disclosure protocol, incorporating biomarker result framing, risk communication, and psychological support, will lead to better comprehension, lower psychological distress, and more appropriate health behaviors in cognitively normal individuals compared to standard, unstructured disclosure.
proposals.method: We will develop a protocol based on a pre-2026 systematic review of risk communication and genetic counseling frameworks. The protocol will be iteratively refined via a Delphi consensus panel of neurologists, neuropsychologists, and ethicists. We will then conduct a randomized controlled trial (RCT) in a cohort of cognitively normal individuals (n=200) seeking biomarker assessment (CSF Aβ42/40, p-tau, synaptic proteins like neurogranin). Participants will be randomized to receive results via the new protocol or standard care. Primary outcomes, assessed at 1 week and 6 months, will include result comprehension (validated questionnaire), psychological impact (Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale), and health behavior changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirically tested, standardized guideline for AD biomarker disclosure in preclinical populations, directly addressing a major ethical and practical challenge in the field. It will generate crucial data on the real-world impact of biomarker knowledge, informing clinical practice and future policy for the ethical implementation of AD prevention strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on biomarker discovery and associations with cognition, this proposal uniquely integrates communication science and clinical ethics with AD biomarker research to create a practical tool for patient care. It moves beyond describing biomarker significance to actively managing its psychosocial and behavioral consequences.

proposals.title: A Parsimonious Diagnostic Signature for Alzheimer's Disease: Integrating Core CSF Amyloid, Tau, and Synaptic Biomarkers with Clinical Scores
proposals.motivation: Current diagnostic frameworks for Alzheimer's disease (AD) increasingly incorporate multiple biomarkers (e.g., Aβ42/40, p-tau, neurofilament light) and clinical assessments, but this complexity is costly and impractical for widespread clinical implementation. There is a critical need to define the minimal, most cost-effective set of features that retains high diagnostic accuracy, especially in the pre-dementia or mild cognitive impairment stages where intervention is most promising.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a minimal signature combining the core CSF biomarkers of amyloidosis (Aβ42/40 ratio) and tauopathy (p-tau181), a key synaptic marker (e.g., neurogranin or SNAP-25), and a brief cognitive memory score will be sufficient to achieve diagnostic accuracy comparable to full multimodal panels in distinguishing AD from non-AD cognitive decline.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing, deeply phenotyped cohorts (e.g., ADNI, BioFINDER) with CSF measures of Aβ42/40, p-tau181, neurogranin, and clinical scores (e.g., MMSE, specific memory domain scores). Using regularized machine learning (LASSO logistic regression) and recursive feature elimination, we will identify the smallest feature subset that maximizes diagnostic accuracy (AUC) for AD (A+T+ by NIA-AA criteria) against controls and other dementias. Model performance will be validated in an independent cohort using bootstrapping and cross-validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, streamlined diagnostic algorithm that reduces testing burden and cost, facilitating broader clinical adoption of biomarker-guided diagnosis. It will also elucidate whether synaptic biomarkers add essential diagnostic information beyond the core AT framework in a real-world, parsimonious model.
proposals.novelty: While individual biomarkers are well-studied, the systematic search for a *minimal sufficient combination* specifically integrating synaptic CSF proteins with core AD pathology and brief cognitive metrics is novel. This moves beyond establishing associations to defining operational diagnostic criteria optimized for practicality.

proposals.title: Identification of CSF Proteomic and Synaptic Biomarker Signatures Associated with Cognitive Resilience in Preclinical Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: A significant proportion of individuals exhibit Alzheimer's disease (AD) pathology, such as amyloid-β aggregation, without manifesting cognitive symptoms, a state termed preclinical AD. Understanding the factors that confer resilience against pathology is a critical, unmet need in dementia research. This study directly addresses this gap by investigating protective biological mechanisms in asymptomatic biomarker-positive individuals, which could inform novel therapeutic strategies aimed at promoting resilience rather than solely removing pathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that asymptomatic individuals with positive AD biomarkers (e.g., CSF Aβ42/40 ratio, p-tau) will exhibit distinct CSF proteomic profiles, characterized by elevated levels of specific synaptic and neuroprotective proteins, compared to both symptomatic AD patients and biomarker-negative controls, and that these protein signatures will correlate with preserved memory performance.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing and newly collected cohorts of cognitively normal older adults, stratified by CSF AD biomarker status (Aβ42/40, p-tau). Using multiplexed immunoassays and targeted mass spectrometry (pre-2026 standard methods), we will quantify a panel of pre-specified synaptic proteins (e.g., neurogranin, GAP-43, synaptotagmin-1) and a broader set of inflammation- and plasticity-related proteins. We will employ multivariate partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) to identify proteomic signatures distinguishing resilient (biomarker+/cognitively normal) from susceptible (biomarker+/impaired) and control groups. Associations between identified protein clusters and detailed neuropsychological memory scores will be tested using linear regression models adjusted for age, sex, and APOE ε4 status.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a cerebrospinal fluid biomarker signature of cognitive resilience in the presence of AD pathology, moving beyond risk factors to identify potential protective mechanisms. It will directly link specific synaptic and other protein levels to preserved cognitive function, providing tangible targets for drug development. The findings will advance the conceptual framework of AD by characterizing the biological basis of resilience, a key component of the AT(N) research framework.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Blennow and others has established associations between synaptic protein CSF levels and memory, this study uniquely applies an 'inverse problem' approach, systematically comparing biomarker-positive individuals with and without symptoms to isolate a resilience-specific signature, rather than a general disease-associated one.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Plasma GFAP Elevations Precede and Predict Regional Tau-PET Accumulation in Cognitively Unimpaired Older Adults
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established plasma glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP) as a marker of astrocyte reactivity and tau-PET as a measure of neurofibrillary tangle pathology, both strongly associated with Alzheimer's disease (AD) progression. However, the temporal and potentially causal relationship between these biomarkers remains unclear, hindering our understanding of whether astrocytic activation is an early driver or a secondary consequence of tau pathology. Clarifying this sequence is critical for identifying the optimal therapeutic window and target for disease-modifying interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that longitudinal increases in plasma GFAP will precede and predict subsequent increases in regional tau-PET signal, specifically in Braak stage-associated regions, supporting a model where astrocyte reactivity is an early event facilitating tau aggregation.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing longitudinal cohorts (e.g., ADNI, BioFINDER) with serial plasma GFAP measurements (Simoa assay) and tau-PET scans ([18F]Flortaucipir) over 4-6 years in cognitively unimpaired older adults. Using linear mixed-effects models, we will model the temporal trajectories of each biomarker. To test temporal precedence, we will implement cross-lagged panel models (CLPM) and bivariate dual-change score models (BDCSM) to assess whether baseline GFAP or its rate of change predicts future tau-PET accumulation, controlling for baseline amyloid status (via PET or CSF Aβ42/40).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal evidence defining the temporal ordering of plasma GFAP and tau-PET changes, clarifying the role of astrocytic reactivity in the AD continuum. It will establish whether plasma GFAP has utility as a proximal, dynamic predictor of impending tau pathology, informing clinical trial design for anti-tau and astrocyte-targeting therapies.
proposals.novelty: While cross-sectional associations between GFAP and tau are known, this study uniquely applies advanced longitudinal causal modeling to disentangle their temporal relationship, moving beyond association to inference of sequence, a critical step for understanding disease mechanisms.

proposals.title: Validation and Characterization of a Left-Lateralized GFAP-Tau Association in Alzheimer's Disease: A Multi-Cohort Neuroimaging and Biofluid Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Blennow and others has established that CSF levels of synaptic proteins (e.g., GAP-43, neurogranin) and glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP) are linked to Alzheimer's disease (AD) pathophysiology and cognitive decline. A recent, intriguing finding suggests a left-lateralized association between plasma GFAP and tau-PET signal, potentially reflecting asymmetric neuroinflammatory responses tied to tau pathology. This finding requires validation in larger, independent cohorts to confirm its robustness and explore its demographic and clinical determinants, which is a critical step before it can be considered a biomarker.
proposals.hypothesis: The left-lateralized association between plasma GFAP and regional tau-PET signal is a replicable phenomenon in independent cohorts and is moderated by factors such as age, sex, genetic risk (APOE ε4 status), and clinical diagnosis along the AD continuum.
proposals.method: We will analyze integrated data from three large, pre-existing cohorts (e.g., ADNI, BioFINDER, others) with available plasma GFAP, tau-PET (e.g., [18F]Flortaucipir), amyloid-PET/CSF, and detailed neuropsychological data. The primary analysis will use multiple linear regression models in cognitively unimpaired and impaired individuals, testing the interaction between plasma GFAP and hemisphere on tau-PET signal in meta-ROIs (e.g., temporal meta-ROI). We will then conduct voxel-wise analyses using SPM12 to map the spatial specificity of the GFAP-tau association. Moderation analyses will test the influence of demographics, APOE status, and amyloid positivity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a definitive assessment of the robustness and generalizability of a novel lateralized glial response in AD. It will clarify whether this signal is a core feature of AD pathophysiology or influenced by specific population characteristics. The findings could inform the development of more precise, pathophysiology-informed biomarkers that account for disease asymmetry.
proposals.novelty: While GFAP is a known marker of astrogliosis, its association with tau pathology has been considered global. Testing and validating a lateralized relationship is a novel concept that challenges the assumption of symmetric neuroinflammatory responses and may link glial activation to the hemispheric asymmetry often observed in AD symptomatology.

proposals.title: Lateralized Astrocytic Activation in Alzheimer's Disease: A Multi-Modal Neuroimaging and CSF Proteomics Investigation of the GFAP-Left Temporal Tau Association
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Kaj Blennow and others has established that cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP), a marker of astrocytic activation, shows a specific correlation with tau pathology in the left temporal lobe, rather than a global association. This lateralized effect is poorly understood but could reflect region-specific vulnerabilities in neuroinflammatory cascades or asymmetric synaptic/network disruption in early Alzheimer's disease (AD). Understanding this asymmetry is critical for refining disease models and developing targeted therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the specific association between CSF GFAP and left temporal tau pathology is driven by (1) a co-localized, left-lateralized increase in neuroinflammation measurable via PET, and (2) a disproportionate disruption of left-hemisphere dominant language and semantic memory networks, linking astrocytic response to clinical phenotype.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing multi-modal data from 150 cognitively unimpaired and mild cognitive impairment participants with CSF measures (GFAP, Aβ42/40, p-tau, NfL, synaptic proteins like neurogranin), [18F]Flortaucipir PET for regional tau, and [11C]PBR28 PET for TSPO as a marker of neuroinflammation. High-resolution 3T MRI will provide structural and resting-state functional connectivity data. Using voxel-based and region-of-interest analyses, we will map the spatial convergence of elevated GFAP, TSPO signal, and tau in the left temporal lobe. We will then employ partial least squares correlation and mediation analyses to test if left-lateralized network dysfunction (e.g., in the default mode and language networks) mediates the link between GFAP and tau.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first in vivo evidence linking a specific CSF astrocyte biomarker to a lateralized pattern of neuroinflammation and tau aggregation. It will clarify whether GFAP primarily reflects a global reactive state or a region-specific pathological process. The findings will advance the biological interpretation of GFAP in AD and inform models of asymmetric disease progression.
proposals.novelty: While GFAP and tau are established biomarkers, their region-specific relationship is unexplored in vivo. This proposal uniquely integrates multi-modal PET imaging of inflammation and tau with CSF proteomics and network-based MRI to mechanistically explain a lateralized biomarker correlation, moving beyond descriptive associations.

proposals.title: Causal inference of the GFAP-tau-cognition pathway in preclinical Alzheimer's disease using structural learning algorithms
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Blennow's work, has established strong associations between CSF biomarkers like glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP), tau, and cognitive decline in Alzheimer's disease (AD). However, the temporal and causal ordering of these events—specifically whether astrocytic activation (GFAP) drives tau pathology or vice versa, and how they jointly impact cognition—remains unclear. Elucidating this directionality is critical for identifying primary therapeutic targets and understanding disease progression in the preclinical phase.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that causal discovery algorithms will reveal a directed pathway where increased CSF GFAP levels precede and causally influence the rise of phosphorylated tau (p-tau), which in turn directly drives early cognitive decline in individuals without dementia.
proposals.method: We will apply structural learning algorithms, specifically the PC algorithm and Greedy Equivalence Search (GES), to high-quality longitudinal CSF biomarker (GFAP, Aβ42/40, p-tau181, NfL) and neuropsychological data from well-characterized cohorts (e.g., ADNI, BioFINDER). These constraint- and score-based algorithms will infer probabilistic causal structures from the observational data. Analyses will be stratified by amyloid status (Aβ+ vs Aβ-) to account for the known initiating role of amyloid, and stability will be assessed using bootstrapping.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a data-driven, causal model of the GFAP-tau-cognition sequence, moving beyond correlation. It will clarify the role of astrocytic reactivity in the tauopathy cascade and identify which biomarker-cognition link is most direct. The findings could prioritize GFAP or p-tau as a interventional target for secondary prevention.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Blennow's group has expertly quantified associations between synaptic proteins, amyloid, and cognition, applying formal causal discovery methods from epidemiology to the GFAP-tau axis represents a novel analytical framework for his biomarker research, aiming to infer directionality rather than just association.

proposals.title: Predicting Asymmetric Tau Pathology from Plasma Biomarkers: A Machine Learning Approach for Lateralization in Alzheimer's Disease
