proposals.motivation: Asymmetric (lateralized) tau deposition, detectable via PET, is a common feature in Alzheimer's disease (AD) and other tauopathies, linked to clinical symptom profiles. Pre-2026 research by Kaj Blennow and others has established plasma glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP) as a sensitive marker of astrocytic response to amyloid-β pathology. However, the relationship between accessible plasma biomarkers and the spatial distribution of tau pathology remains unexplored, limiting the use of blood tests for predicting disease heterogeneity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a panel of plasma biomarkers, including GFAP, neurofilament light (NfL), and amyloid-β 42/40 ratio, contains sufficient information to predict the lateralization (left vs. right hemispheric predominance) of tau pathology as measured by [18F]flortaucipir PET.
proposals.method: We will use a cohort of individuals across the AD continuum with concurrent plasma biomarker profiles and quantitative tau-PET data. Hemisphere-specific tau SUVr values will be calculated to define a lateralization index (LI). We will train and compare multiple ML classifiers (e.g., Support Vector Machines, Random Forests, and Gradient Boosting Machines) to predict a binary (left/right) or continuous LI outcome from the plasma features. Model performance will be evaluated using nested cross-validation, with feature importance analysis (e.g., SHAP values) to identify key predictors.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish a proof-of-concept for predicting tau lateralization from blood, moving beyond simple biomarker levels to spatial pathology. It will provide novel insights into the biological correlates of asymmetric disease progression. If successful, it could inform more targeted patient stratification for clinical trials and help explain clinical heterogeneity using minimally invasive methods.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has linked plasma GFAP to amyloid burden, no study has attempted to predict the spatial distribution of tau pathology from plasma. Applying ML to this specific prediction task for lateralization, rather than just diagnosis or staging, represents a novel integration of fluid biomarkers and neuroimaging phenotypes.

proposals.title: Modeling Astrocytic Activation in Alzheimer's Disease: A Machine Learning Framework to Predict CSF GFAP from Tau-PET Topography and Synaptic Biomarkers
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research establishes that tau pathology and neuroinflammation, particularly astrocytic activation marked by glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP), are core features of Alzheimer's disease (AD) progression. However, the spatial and temporal relationship between aggregated tau (measured by PET) and reactive astrogliosis (measured in CSF) remains poorly quantified. Developing a model to predict GFAP from tau PET would provide a non-invasive tool to infer a key inflammatory process, crucial for understanding disease mechanisms and patient stratification.
proposals.hypothesis: Regional tau-PET signal patterns, combined with core AD CSF biomarkers (e.g., Aβ42/40, p-tau) and synaptic markers (e.g., neurogranin, GAP-43), can accurately predict CSF GFAP levels, reflecting a predictable relationship between tau deposition topography and astrocytic response.
proposals.method: We will use data from well-characterized cohorts (e.g., ADNI, BioFINDER) with concurrent tau-PET (e.g., [18F]Flortaucipir), CSF GFAP, and other biomarkers. Regional tau-PET SUVRs from a standardized atlas will serve as primary features. We will employ elastic net regression for feature selection and baseline modeling, followed by more complex non-linear algorithms like gradient boosting machines (e.g., XGBoost) or a multilayer perceptron to capture interactions. Model performance will be evaluated using cross-validated R² and mean squared error for predicting continuous log-transformed GFAP.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative model linking in vivo tau topography to a central CSF inflammatory marker. It will clarify which tau-affected brain regions are most strongly associated with astrocytic reactivity. The model could serve as a template for inferring inflammatory states from widely available tau-PET, enhancing pathophysiological interpretation in clinical trials and diagnostic settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Blennow and others has established associations between individual biomarkers, the inverse problem of predicting a dynamic CSF inflammatory protein from a static neuroimaging pattern of protein aggregation is novel. The integrative approach using synaptic health markers to refine the prediction model moves beyond simple correlation to a systems-level understanding.

proposals.title: Predicting Domain-Specific Cognitive Decline from Multi-Regional Amyloid-PET and Synaptic CSF Biomarker Profiles in Preclinical and Prodromal Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: While amyloid-β (Aβ) pathology is a core feature of Alzheimer's disease (AD), its relationship to specific clinical symptoms is highly variable. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Blennow's work, shows that synaptic CSF proteins (e.g., neurogranin, SNAP-25) correlate with memory performance, but integrated models using detailed Aβ topography and synaptic markers to predict specific cognitive domain trajectories are lacking. This limits personalized prognosis and targeted trial enrollment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combination of regional amyloid-PET load (e.g., from frontal, posterior cingulate, lateral temporal regions) and CSF levels of synaptic proteins will predict the rate and pattern of decline in specific cognitive domains (e.g., episodic memory vs. executive function) better than global amyloid measures alone.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal data from cohorts like ADNI, including participants with baseline amyloid-PET (quantified regionally using standardized uptake value ratios), CSF Aβ42/40, p-tau, and synaptic markers (neurogranin, SNAP-25, GAP-43), and annual cognitive assessments. We will employ elastic net regression and random forest models to identify biomarker combinations predictive of 2-3 year cognitive slope in specific domains. Model performance will be validated in a held-out test set, assessing accuracy (R²) and clinical utility via ROC curves for predicting meaningful decline.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clinically actionable model mapping biomarker profiles to likely symptomatic trajectories, moving beyond generic 'amyloid positivity.' It will clarify which biomarker patterns are associated with predominant memory versus non-memory decline. The findings could inform the design of more targeted clinical trials by enabling stratification based on predicted clinical phenotype.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior studies linking global biomarker levels to general cognition, this proposal explicitly formulates an inverse problem, using high-dimensional biomarker inputs (multi-regional Aβ plus synaptic markers) to predict specific cognitive domain outcomes, integrating Dr. Blennow's expertise in synaptic CSF markers with advanced neuroimaging features.

proposals.title: Synaptic Tau Accumulation and Synaptic Dysfunction: A Causal Mediation Analysis in Preclinical Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: Synaptic loss is the strongest pathological correlate of cognitive decline in Alzheimer's disease (AD). While soluble tau aggregates, including synaptic tau aggregates (STAs), are implicated in synaptic dysfunction, it remains unclear whether STAs are the primary toxic species or if their presence triggers downstream pathological cascades (e.g., neuroinflammation, oxidative stress) that are the direct mediators of synaptic damage. Disentangling this is critical for developing targeted therapeutics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the association between CSF levels of synaptic tau and synaptic dysfunction (measured by CSF synaptic protein levels) is not direct but is significantly mediated by secondary neuroinflammatory and neurodegenerative processes.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal data from the Swedish BioFINDER-2 cohort, focusing on cognitively unimpaired individuals with amyloid pathology. Using established immunoassays, we will measure CSF levels of STA-associated tau fragments (e.g., MTBR-tau243), pre-synaptic (e.g., synaptotagmin-1) and post-synaptic (e.g., neurogranin) proteins, and markers of neuroinflammation (e.g., GFAP, sTREM2) and neurodegeneration (e.g., NfL). We will employ causal mediation analysis using structural equation modeling to quantify the proportion of the effect of STA levels on synaptic protein levels that is mediated through these secondary pathways.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel mechanistic insight into the causal pathway of tau-induced synaptic injury. It will clarify whether therapeutic strategies should target STAs directly or their downstream effectors. The findings will refine the tau toxicity model in AD and inform the selection of biomarkers for clinical trials targeting synaptic preservation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work establishes associations between tau and synaptic biomarkers, this study applies a formal causal inference framework to test a specific mechanistic model of indirect toxicity, moving beyond correlation to proposed causation within the pre-2026 analytical toolkit.

proposals.title: Amyloid-β Pathology Modulates the Association Between Cerebrospinal Fluid pS262/pS356 Tau and Synaptic Dysfunction in Preclinical Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) biomarkers of synaptic dysfunction, such as pS262/pS356 tau (STA), are promising for tracking early Alzheimer's disease (AD) progression. However, the precise relationship between amyloid-β (Aβ) pathology, the earliest AD hallmark, and STA formation remains unclear. Understanding whether STA is an independent marker or is mechanistically modulated by Aβ is critical for accurate disease staging and for developing Aβ-targeted therapies that aim to preserve synaptic function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Aβ pathology, measured by CSF Aβ42/40 ratio, modulates the relationship between CSF pS262/pS356 tau levels and markers of synaptic degeneration (e.g., neurogranin, SNAP-25), such that the association between STA and synaptic markers is strongest in the presence of significant Aβ pathology.
proposals.method: We will analyze cross-sectional and longitudinal CSF samples from well-characterized cohorts (e.g., ADNI, BioFINDER) of cognitively unimpaired individuals and those with mild cognitive impairment. Using established ELISA or SIMOA platforms, we will measure Aβ42/40, pS262/pS356 tau, and synaptic proteins (neurogranin, SNAP-25). Statistical analyses will employ multiple linear regression and moderation analysis, testing for an interaction term between Aβ status (positive/negative by established cut-offs) and STA levels on synaptic protein levels, adjusting for age, sex, and total tau.
proposals.contribution: This work will clarify the pathophysiological interplay between Aβ and specific tau phosphorylation at the synapse, informing the biological interpretation of the STA biomarker. It will provide evidence on whether STA can serve as an Aβ-dependent indicator of synaptic injury, refining biomarker models for preclinical AD. The findings could guide the selection of outcome measures for clinical trials targeting Aβ.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Blennow and others has established associations between synaptic CSF proteins and memory, and between Aβ and choroidal abnormalities, the specific hypothesis that Aβ pathology moderates the STA-synaptic injury relationship is novel and untested. This moves beyond simple correlation to test a mechanistic interaction central to the amyloid cascade hypothesis.

proposals.title: Longitudinal validation of CSF pS262/pS356 as a predictor of cognitive decline and tau pathology progression in preclinical and prodromal Alzheimer's disease
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the Alzheimer's disease field urgently needs fluid biomarkers that can track the progression of tau pathology, particularly neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs), in living individuals. While CSF amyloid-β and total/phospho-tau (e.g., p-tau181) are established diagnostic markers, their ability to predict the rate of future cognitive decline and NFT accumulation longitudinally is limited. A novel assay measuring tau phosphorylated at sites S262 and S356 (pS262/pS356), which is highly specific for pathological tau aggregation, has shown promise cross-sectionally but requires validation in longitudinal cohorts.
proposals.hypothesis: Higher baseline levels of CSF pS262/pS356 will predict a faster rate of future cognitive decline and a greater increase in established tau PET tracer binding (e.g., [18F]flortaucipir) over time in individuals without dementia, independent of baseline amyloid status.
proposals.method: We will utilize existing longitudinal samples and data from well-characterized clinical cohorts (e.g., ADNI, BioFINDER). CSF pS262/pS356 levels will be measured at baseline using the established Single Molecule Array (Simoa) immunoassay. Longitudinal outcomes will include annual change in composite memory scores and, in a subset, change in tau PET SUVRs in Braak-stage regions. Statistical analyses will employ linear mixed-effects models to assess the association between baseline pS262/pS356 and rates of decline, adjusting for age, sex, APOE ε4 status, and baseline amyloid PET/CSF Aβ42. We will also test for interaction effects between pS262/pS356 and amyloid status.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide critical evidence for the utility of CSF pS262/pS356 as a prognostic biomarker for disease progression in Alzheimer's continuum. It will directly link a specific synaptic-related tau phosphorylation signature to clinical and pathological outcomes over time, offering a tool for enriching clinical trials with individuals likely to progress faster. The findings will enhance the understanding of the temporal dynamics between different tau epitopes and symptom onset.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Blennow and others has shown cross-sectional associations between synaptic protein CSF levels and memory, this proposal uniquely tests the predictive, longitudinal validity of a specific tau phosphorylation assay (pS262/pS356) tied directly to NFT pathology against both cognitive and in vivo tau PET trajectories.

proposals.title: A multi-analyte CSF panel of synaptic and neurodegenerative proteins for staging disease severity in biomarker-confirmed Alzheimer's disease
proposals.motivation: While CSF biomarkers like Aβ42/40 and p-tau are excellent for diagnosing Alzheimer's disease (AD), their utility for staging disease severity and tracking progression within AD patients is limited. There is a critical unmet need for fluid biomarkers that reflect synaptic integrity and neurodegeneration to monitor disease course, which is essential for clinical trial enrichment and evaluating therapeutic efficacy. Pre-2026 work, including by Blennow and colleagues, has shown that CSF levels of synaptic proteins like neurogranin and GAP-43 correlate with cognitive performance, but their combined value for staging within a confirmed AD cohort is underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-analyte CSF panel combining pre-synaptic (e.g., GAP-43), post-synaptic (e.g., neurogranin), and general neurodegenerative (e.g., NfL) markers will outperform established core AD biomarkers (Aβ42/40, p-tau) in correlating with and predicting the severity of cognitive impairment and brain atrophy in individuals with biomarker-confirmed AD.
proposals.method: We will analyze CSF from a well-characterized cohort of participants with AD (Aβ-positive, per pre-2026 NIA-AA criteria), spanning the clinical spectrum from preclinical to dementia. Using established, validated ELISA and SIMOA assays available pre-2026, we will measure Aβ42/40, p-tau181, NfL, neurogranin, GAP-43, and SNAP-25. We will employ multivariate linear regression and machine learning (specifically, Random Forest regression and gradient boosting models) to identify the optimal combination of biomarkers for predicting continuous cognitive scores (e.g., MMSE, CDR-SB) and MRI-derived hippocampal volume. Model performance will be evaluated using cross-validation and compared to models using only core AD biomarkers.
proposals.contribution: This study will define a novel CSF biomarker signature for staging AD severity, moving beyond diagnostic classification. It will provide a validated, multi-dimensional fluid tool for stratifying patients in clinical trials based on projected disease trajectory. The findings will strengthen the biological link between in-vivo synaptic CSF measures and the clinical and anatomical progression of AD.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the dominant biomarker paradigm from case-control discrimination to intra-disease staging within a rigorously defined AD population. The novelty lies in the systematic head-to-head comparison and integration of synaptic and axonal markers against core AD pathology markers specifically for severity staging, rather than diagnosis.

proposals.title: Comparative analysis of phosphorylated tau biomarkers in plasma, saliva, and capillary blood: associations with amyloid status and cognitive performance in a memory clinic cohort
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, plasma phosphorylated tau (p-tau) assays are revolutionizing Alzheimer's disease (AD) diagnostics, but blood sampling remains a barrier for truly scalable, remote, or repeated screening. The potential of saliva or capillary blood (from finger-prick) as less invasive, patient-administered matrices for p-tau measurement is largely unexplored, despite their clear logistical advantages for large-scale studies and primary care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that p-tau levels measured in saliva and capillary blood will correlate strongly with established plasma p-tau concentrations, and that these correlations will be strongest in individuals with cerebral amyloid-β pathology, reflecting their common central origin.
proposals.method: We will recruit 200 memory clinic patients (cognitively unimpaired to mild dementia). Paired samples of venous plasma (reference), self-collected capillary blood (via Tasso-SST device), and saliva (via Oragene kit) will be obtained. p-tau217 and p-tau181 will be measured using the leading pre-2026 immunoassays (e.g., Lilly p-tau217, Quanterix p-tau181). Amyloid status will be determined via CSF Aβ42/40 or amyloid-PET. Statistical analysis will involve Spearman correlations, Bland-Altman plots, and multivariable regression models to assess the agreement between matrices and their independent associations with amyloid positivity and cognitive scores (e.g., MMSE, memory domain tests).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, head-to-head comparison of p-tau biomarkers across three key peripheral matrices. It will establish the feasibility and diagnostic concordance of saliva and capillary blood p-tau, directly informing the design of future decentralized clinical trials and population screening programs. It will also advance the understanding of synaptic and axonal biomarker trafficking into different biofluids.
proposals.novelty: While plasma p-tau is well-established, systematic comparison with saliva and capillary blood for specific p-tau isoforms (217, 181) in a well-characterized cohort is novel. The focus on self-collection methods directly addresses a critical translational gap not yet tackled by major biomarker consortia as of 2025.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of Cross-Platform Harmonization Equations for Cerebrospinal Fluid Phosphorylated Tau Assays in Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: Phosphorylated tau (p-tau) in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) is a core biomarker for Alzheimer's disease (AD) pathology, but clinical and research use is hampered by variability between high-performing immunoassays (e.g., Elecsys, Simoa, MSD). This lack of standardization impedes the comparison of results across studies, the establishment of universal diagnostic cut-offs, and the pooling of data for large-scale collaborative research. Harmonization is a critical unmet need to translate biomarker findings into consistent clinical practice and accelerate therapeutic trials.
proposals.hypothesis: Robust, platform-specific mathematical conversion equations can be derived to accurately align CSF p-tau concentrations measured by different assays, enabling the creation of harmonized values that reflect the true underlying biological signal.
proposals.method: We will use CSF samples from well-characterized cohorts, including cognitively unimpaired individuals and patients with AD and other dementias, with measurements from at least three major p-tau assays (e.g., Elecsys p-tau181/217, Simoa p-tau181/217, MSD p-tau181). Using pairwise comparisons, we will apply Deming regression to account for measurement error in both assays. We will then develop and compare harmonization models, including simple linear equations and more advanced approaches like Passing-Bablok regression or quantile normalization. The performance of the conversion equations will be validated in an independent cohort by assessing their ability to preserve associations with amyloid PET status, clinical diagnosis, and cognitive scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the field with validated, open-source conversion factors to directly translate p-tau results between major analytical platforms. It will enable the meta-analysis of existing and future biomarker studies, facilitate the establishment of globally applicable reference limits, and improve the consistency of p-tau-based participant stratification in multi-center clinical trials.
proposals.novelty: While method comparison studies exist, this proposal focuses on creating practical, validated harmonization tools for immediate application. It integrates multiple high-performance assays beyond simple pairwise correlation, aiming for a unified framework that accounts for the non-linear relationships often observed at extreme biomarker concentrations.

proposals.title: Systematic Identification of Biological and Technical Confounders Attenuating Cerebrospinal Fluid Phosphorylated Tau Fold-Change in Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) phosphorylated tau (p-tau) is a core biomarker for Alzheimer's disease (AD) neuropathology, yet its observed fold-change between AD and control groups shows substantial variability across studies. This variability obscures the true biological signal, complicates clinical interpretation, and may limit the utility of p-tau for early detection and staging. Identifying the factors that systematically diminish this effect size is critical for refining biomarker accuracy and understanding disease heterogeneity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific pre-analytical variables, comorbid pathologies, and non-AD synaptic degeneration processes act as significant confounders, reducing the observed CSF p-tau fold-change in AD cohorts compared to cognitively unimpaired controls.
proposals.method: We will perform a multi-cohort meta-analysis of existing CSF biomarker datasets (e.g., ADNI, BioFINDER, others) with harmonized clinical and biomarker data. Using pre-specified linear mixed-effects models, we will test the attenuation of the AD diagnosis effect on p-tau levels when sequentially adding candidate confounders as covariates. Key confounders include renal function (creatinine), blood-brain barrier integrity (CSF/serum albumin ratio), concomitant vascular pathology (CSF neurofilament light), non-AD synaptic markers (e.g., CSF SNAP-25, GAP-43), and pre-analytical factors (sample storage time, tube type). Machine learning (LASSO regression) will be used for exploratory, data-driven identification of additional attenuating factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a ranked list of factors that most significantly confound CSF p-tau levels, providing clear guidance for covariate adjustment in future studies and clinical trials. It will enhance the diagnostic precision of p-tau by defining subpopulations where its interpretability is limited and may reveal novel biological interactions between tau pathology and other degenerative processes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies correlates of p-tau, this study uniquely focuses on factors that specifically *diminish* its case-control effect size, applying a formal confounder-assessment framework. It integrates Blennow's expertise in synaptic CSF markers with amyloid/tau pathology to test if non-AD synaptic loss is a key, underappreciated attenuator of the p-tau signal.

proposals.title: Modeling the Inferred Rate of Plasma p-tau217 Change from a Single Timepoint as a Superior Predictor of Cognitive Decline in Preclinical Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: Plasma phosphorylated tau (p-tau217) is a promising biomarker for Alzheimer's disease (AD) pathology, but clinical interpretation relies on longitudinal sampling to measure change, which is impractical in many settings. A static, single-timepoint measurement may not capture the dynamic, individual-specific trajectory of pathology accumulation, which is likely more closely tied to imminent cognitive decline. Developing a method to infer this rate from a single sample would dramatically increase the prognostic utility of plasma biomarkers in primary care and clinical trials.
proposals.hypothesis: The inferred rate of change of plasma p-tau217, modeled from a single measurement using a population-based Bayesian framework, provides a more accurate prognostic signal for near-term cognitive and synaptic decline than the absolute p-tau217 concentration alone.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing longitudinal cohorts (e.g., BioFINDER, ADNI) with serial plasma p-tau217, amyloid-PET, and cognitive data. Using non-linear mixed-effects modeling (e.g., Gompertz growth curve), we will characterize the population-average trajectory of p-tau217 accumulation relative to amyloid positivity and age. For each individual with only a baseline plasma p-tau217 value, we will use Empirical Bayes estimation to infer their posterior distribution of the personal accumulation rate. This inferred rate will be tested against longitudinal cognitive scores and CSF synaptic biomarkers (e.g., neurogranin, GAP-43) using linear mixed models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, clinically feasible metric—an inferred rate of pathology accumulation—that enhances risk stratification from a single blood draw. It will directly bridge Blennow's expertise in synaptic CSF biomarkers with emerging plasma p-tau assays, offering a dynamic model of disease progression. The method could transform the use of plasma biomarkers from a cross-sectional diagnostic tool into a personalized prognostic instrument.
proposals.novelty: While others model longitudinal change from multiple timepoints, this approach uniquely posits that the *rate* can be inferred from a *single* measurement using prior population knowledge, challenging the necessity of repeated sampling for prognostic estimation. It moves beyond simple associations of amyloid and p-tau levels to model the dynamic process linking them to synaptic and cognitive outcomes.

proposals.title: Mapping the Phase Landscape of Alzheimer's Pathology: Testing for Metastable States and Reversibility in Protein Aggregation
proposals.motivation: Current Alzheimer's disease (AD) models are dominated by a linear, sequential cascade of pathology, from amyloid-beta (Aβ) to tau to neurodegeneration. This framework has failed to yield effective disease-modifying therapies, suggesting a need for a paradigm shift. The analogy from physics, where systems can occupy multiple metastable states and transition non-linearly under specific perturbations, offers a novel conceptual model for understanding the progression and potential reversibility of AD hallmarks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that established AD pathological states (e.g., soluble Aβ oligomers, plaques, tau tangles) represent metastable phases in a complex molecular energy landscape, and that targeted manipulation of specific molecular 'energy barriers'—such as synaptic protein homeostasis or lipid metabolism—can induce regression or reordering of these pathological states.
proposals.method: We will first construct a computational phase-field model of Aβ and tau aggregation, parameterized with pre-2026 kinetic data from CSF (e.g., synaptic protein levels, Aβ42/40 ratios) to define potential energy minima. Experimentally, we will use established cellular and transgenic mouse models (e.g., APP/PS1, Tau P301S) to test perturbations predicted to lower barriers between states. These will include pharmacological modulation of the integrated stress response (ISR) and ATP-dependent chaperone networks (e.g., Hsp70/90), as well as lipid bilayer composition via dietary or genetic manipulation (e.g., PLD3). Outcomes will be longitudinally assessed via high-resolution imaging of plaque/tangle burden and synaptosomal preparations for synaptic protein profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel theoretical and experimental framework for AD progression, moving beyond the linear cascade model. It will identify specific, targetable molecular systems that act as 'switches' between pathological states, directly informing new therapeutic strategies aimed at disease reversal rather than mere slowing of progression.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a physics-based phase transition framework to AD pathology, explicitly testing for reversibility and non-sequential state changes—a radical departure from the entrenched amyloid cascade hypothesis. It integrates Dr. Blennow's expertise in CSF biomarkers of synaptic integrity (e.g., neurogranin, SNAP-25) as quantifiers of the system's state within this new model.

proposals.title: Non-Linear Dynamics and Reversibility of Alzheimer's Disease Biomarker Trajectories: A Longitudinal CSF Proteomic Study
proposals.motivation: The dominant model of Alzheimer's disease (AD) progression, such as the amyloid/tau/neurodegeneration (ATN) framework, posits a linear, sequential cascade of pathological events. However, this model fails to explain significant clinical heterogeneity, including periods of cognitive stability or even improvement in some individuals. Investigating the potential for non-linearity or reversibility in AD biomarkers is crucial for understanding disease mechanisms and identifying targets for therapeutic intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that key CSF biomarkers of amyloid-β pathology, tau pathology, and synaptic integrity do not follow a strictly linear, unidirectional progression in the years preceding clinical dementia. Instead, their trajectories can exhibit non-linear fluctuations or even partial reversibility, which are associated with changes in cognitive performance.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal CSF samples from well-characterized cohorts (e.g., ADNI, BioFINDER) of individuals across the cognitive spectrum (cognitively unimpaired, MCI, AD dementia). Using established immunoassays, we will measure serial levels of Aβ42/40, p-tau181, and synaptic proteins (e.g., neurogranin, GAP-43, SNAP-25). We will apply non-linear mixed-effects models and latent class trajectory analysis to identify distinct temporal patterns of biomarker change. Statistical mediation and cross-lagged panel models will be used to test for bidirectional relationships between biomarker levels and memory scores over time.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal, multi-protein evidence for or against the linearity of AD biomarker progression. It will identify subgroups with distinct biomarker trajectories, potentially revealing a subset with reversible synaptic pathology. The findings could redefine biological staging models and highlight synaptic biomarkers as sensitive indicators of dynamic disease activity and therapeutic response.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has established cross-sectional associations, this proposal directly tests the core assumption of linear progression using high-frequency longitudinal data and advanced statistical modeling of pre-2026 biomarkers. It uniquely integrates established amyloid/tau markers with Dr. Blennow's expertise in synaptic CSF proteins to model their inter-relationships dynamically over time.

proposals.title: Longitudinal CSF Synaptic Protein and Amyloid-β Dynamics in Preclinical Alzheimer's Disease: A High-Risk Cohort Study Using Within-Subject Change-Point Analysis
proposals.motivation: A critical unresolved question in Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the precise temporal sequence of biomarker change relative to the emergence of the earliest cognitive symptoms. Current models, based on cross-sectional data and binary thresholds, cannot determine if subtle biomarker fluctuations precede or follow incipient subjective cognitive decline (SCD), a key risk state. Resolving this is essential for accurate staging and timely intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: In cognitively unimpaired individuals at high genetic risk for AD, within-subject deviations in CSF levels of synaptic proteins (e.g., neurogranin, SNAP-25) and amyloid-β42/40 ratio will be detectable concurrent with or prior to the onset of validated, persistent subjective cognitive complaints, challenging the notion of a long, purely biological preclinical phase.
proposals.method: We will establish a prospective cohort of 200 APOE ε4 homozygotes aged 50-65, cognitively normal at baseline. Participants will undergo quarterly remote digital cognitive testing and SCD assessments, and biannual lumbar punctures for ultra-sensitive measurement of CSF amyloid-β42/40, p-tau181, and synaptic proteins (neurogranin, SNAP-25, GAP-43). The core analysis will apply Bayesian online change-point detection algorithms (e.g., Pruned Exact Linear Time) to each individual's longitudinal biomarker and symptom data streams to identify the precise timing of significant within-subject deviations from their personal baseline.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution, within-subject map of the temporal dynamics between synaptic injury, amyloid pathology, and symptom onset in the preclinical phase. It will define whether SCD represents a concurrent clinical correlate of initial biomarker change, refining the AT(N) framework. The findings will directly inform the design of secondary prevention trials by identifying the optimal biomarker-clinical window for intervention.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond group-level comparisons and static abnormality thresholds, this study uses an intensive, prospective design and within-subject change-point modeling to resolve the temporal precedence question at the individual level, directly integrating fluid synaptic biomarkers with structured SCD phenotyping.

proposals.title: Dissociation of Clinical Impairment from Core CSF Biomarkers in Preclinical and Prodromal Alzheimer's Disease: A Focus on Synaptic and Neuroinflammatory Pathways
proposals.motivation: The prevailing AT(N) framework posits a temporal sequence where biomarker changes precede clinical symptoms. However, clinical cohorts reveal individuals with significant cognitive complaints or impairment despite normal cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) amyloid-β and tau levels. This discordance challenges diagnostic models and suggests either non-AD pathologies or alternative, biomarker-silent neurodegenerative mechanisms are at play, which must be understood to improve early diagnosis and intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of individuals with significant subjective cognitive decline or mild cognitive impairment, but with CSF Aβ42 and p-tau levels within the normal range, will exhibit distinct CSF proteomic profiles characterized by pronounced synaptic dysfunction and neuroinflammation, explaining their clinical phenotype.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing and newly collected CSF samples from well-characterized cohorts (e.g., ADNI, BioFINDER). Participants will be stratified into four groups based on clinical status (cognitively unimpaired vs. impaired) and AT biomarker status (positive vs. negative). Using established, pre-2026 multiplex immunoassays (e.g., Luminex, Ella), we will measure a panel of pre-specified synaptic proteins (e.g., neurogranin, SNAP-25, GAP-43, synaptotagmin-1) and neuroinflammatory markers (e.g., sTREM2, GFAP, YKL-40). Statistical analyses will employ multivariate logistic regression and partial least squares discriminant analysis to identify proteomic signatures distinguishing the clinically impaired/AT-negative group.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence for a biomarker-negative clinical phenotype in the Alzheimer's continuum, potentially defining a new subgroup. It will directly link this phenotype to specific synaptic and inflammatory CSF profiles, moving beyond the core AT(N) biomarkers. The findings could refine diagnostic criteria and identify novel therapeutic targets for individuals not captured by current amyloid/tau-centric models.
proposals.novelty: While synaptic CSF markers are known to correlate with cognition, their systematic evaluation in a specifically defined cohort of clinically impaired individuals with normal Aβ and tau pathology is novel. This approach directly tests the limits of the AT(N) framework by seeking the biological basis of symptoms when canonical biomarkers are absent.

proposals.title: Causal Network Modeling of Neuropathological Copathologies in Alzheimer's Disease: Distinguishing Drivers from Passengers
proposals.motivation: Mixed neuropathologies are the rule, not the exception, in dementia, but their causal interplay remains poorly understood. Determining whether copathologies like TDP-43 or alpha-synuclein are independent drivers or secondary passengers of the primary Alzheimer's disease (AD) process is critical for accurate diagnosis, prognosis, and the development of targeted therapies. This mirrors the historical challenge in cancer genomics of distinguishing driver from passenger mutations, a problem addressed with causal computational frameworks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of the variance in clinical outcomes attributed to common copathologies in AD can be explained by the severity and stage of primary AD pathology (amyloid-β and tau), and that causal network modeling will reveal these copathologies as reactive epiphenomena rather than independent causal drivers.
proposals.method: We will apply causal discovery algorithms, specifically the PC (Peter-Clark) and FCI (Fast Causal Inference) algorithms, to high-dimensional neuropathological data from large, well-characterized brain bank cohorts (e.g., ADNI Neuropathology, NACC). Input variables will include quantitative measures of Aβ plaques, neurofibrillary tangle staging (Braak), TDP-43, alpha-synuclein, vascular pathology, and clinical/cognitive scores. The analysis will construct probabilistic graphical models (Bayesian networks) to infer directed dependencies, followed by regression analyses to test if copathologies retain predictive power for cognitive decline after conditioning on AD pathology stage.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven causal map of neuropathological interactions in AD, moving beyond descriptive co-occurrence. It will directly inform the biological interpretation of copathology biomarkers in living patients, such as those in CSF, by clarifying their likely causal status. The findings could refine disease subtypes and prognostic models by identifying which pathological combinations represent truly independent disease drivers.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in adapting formal causal inference methods, well-established in genomics, to the problem of neuropathological deconvolution in AD. It moves beyond assessing simple correlations or additive effects to explicitly model directional relationships and conditional dependencies between pathologies.

proposals.title: Longitudinal trajectories of synaptic integrity and cognitive resilience in individuals with discordant AT(N) and clinical stages
proposals.motivation: The AT(N) framework has revealed individuals with discordant biological and clinical stages, such as amyloid-positive (A+) individuals with normal cognition (asymptomatic Alzheimer's disease) or amyloid-negative (A-) individuals with cognitive impairment (SNAP). The long-term outcomes and the biological factors, particularly synaptic integrity, that determine progression or resilience in these groups are poorly understood. This is critical for refining prognostic models and identifying protective mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that longitudinal changes in CSF synaptic protein levels (e.g., neurogranin, SNAP-25, GAP-43) will differ between stage-discordant groups and will more accurately predict subsequent cognitive decline and conversion to dementia than amyloid status alone.
proposals.method: We will longitudinally analyze existing cohort data (e.g., ADNI, BioFINDER) from individuals classified as A+N-/A-N+ (SNAP), A+N- (asymptomatic AD), and A-N- (controls). Using linear mixed-effects models, we will track annual rates of change in CSF synaptic biomarkers, amyloid-PET, tau-PET, and cognitive scores (e.g., MMSE, CDR-SB). Cox proportional hazards models will assess the risk of clinical progression, with synaptic marker slopes as time-dependent covariates. Machine learning (random forest) will identify baseline profiles predictive of resilience.
proposals.contribution: This study will define the temporal dynamics of synaptic injury in relation to amyloid and tau pathology across discordant stages, providing a biological explanation for clinical resilience or vulnerability. It will establish whether synaptic biomarkers offer superior prognostic value for individual risk stratification, informing clinical trial enrichment and personalized monitoring strategies.
proposals.novelty: While cross-sectional synaptic-CSF studies exist, a longitudinal analysis focused specifically on stage-discordant individuals is novel. It moves beyond static staging to model the dynamic interaction between pathology, synaptic health, and clinical outcome in these enigmatic groups.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Causal Mediation of Copathologies in the Discordance Between Tau Pathology and Clinical Severity in Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: A significant clinical challenge in Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the frequent discordance between the severity of canonical neuropathology (e.g., Braak tau stage) and ante-mortem cognitive status. This discordance is attributed to mixed pathologies, but the specific, independent contribution of each copathology (e.g., TDP-43, alpha-synuclein, vascular disease) to this divergence remains poorly quantified. Understanding which copathologies are the most potent drivers of clinical decline is crucial for accurate prognosis and developing targeted, combination therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific copathologies act as causal mediators, differentially explaining the variance in clinical severity not accounted for by tau pathology alone, and that their mediating effects can be ranked to identify the most impactful drivers of stage discordance.
proposals.method: We will apply a formal causal mediation analysis framework to data from well-characterized longitudinal cohorts with ante-mortem cognitive assessments and comprehensive post-mortem neuropathology. The primary exposure will be Braak tau stage, the outcome will be a validated global cognitive score proximate to death, and binary/categorical mediators will include TDP-43 pathology, Lewy body pathology, cerebrovascular disease, and hippocampal sclerosis. We will use the counterfactual-based mediation models (e.g., using the 'mediation' package in R) to decompose the total effect of tau stage on cognitive score into direct and indirect effects mediated through each copathology, adjusting for key confounders like age, sex, and APOE genotype.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal-model-based quantification of the proportion of clinical discordance attributable to specific copathologies, moving beyond associative studies. The results will yield a ranked, etiologically informative profile of mixed pathology impact, offering a novel framework for patient stratification and clarifying which non-tau pathways are most critical to target for preserving cognition in AD.
proposals.novelty: While copathologies are known to be associated with worse outcomes, applying a formal causal mediation framework from epidemiology to neuropathology data is novel, allowing for the decomposition of total effects into pathway-specific contributions and providing a quantitative, rather than merely qualitative, understanding of their roles.

ideas.title: Subject-Specific Vulnerability-Prior Bayesian Model for Estimating Regional Brain Pathology from Plasma Biomarkers
ideas.description: This research develops a Bayesian modeling framework to estimate subject-specific regional amyloid/tau pathology from plasma biomarkers. The core innovation is the integration of a personalized neurobiological vulnerability prior, derived from each individual's baseline structural MRI, vascular health indicators, and genetic risk profile, which accounts for inter-subject heterogeneity in pathology deposition. This prior constrains a probabilistic inverse model that uses plasma biomarker levels to update the estimated regional burden map. The system is designed to produce more accurate and biologically plausible estimates than direct mapping approaches by explicitly modeling the subject-specific factors that modulate the relationship between systemic biomarker levels and focal brain pathology.

ideas.title: Decomposing the Impact of Copathologies on Stage Discordance Using a Causal Mediation Framework
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from epidemiology—causal mediation analysis for multifactorial disease outcomes—to the problem of stage discordance in neurodegenerative disease. In public health, this method is used to decompose the total effect of a primary risk factor into the portions mediated by specific, distinct pathways. Here, we would apply this statistical framework to quantify how individual copathologies (like TDP-43, vascular disease, alpha-synuclein, or hippocampal sclerosis) differentially contribute to the discordance between neuropathological staging (e.g., Braak tau stage) and ante-mortem clinical severity. By treating each copathology as a potential mediator, the analysis can estimate the proportion of observed clinical discordance specifically attributable to each pathology's independent effect. This would move beyond simple correlation to provide a causal-model-based ranking of which copathologies are the most potent drivers of divergence between pathology and symptoms, offering targeted insights for patient stratification and therapeutic development.

ideas.title: Deconvolving Copathologies: A Causal Network Approach Adapted from Cancer Genomics
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that measured copathologies in Alzheimer's disease (e.g., TDP-43, alpha-synuclein, vascular disease) are independent contributors by examining if they are epiphenomena of the primary AD biological process. The analogy is drawn from cancer genomics, where a similar problem of distinguishing independent driver mutations from secondary passenger mutations is solved using causal inference and probabilistic graphical models on multi-omics data to deconvolve the sequence and dependencies of molecular events. Adapted to AD, the research would apply causal network modeling to high-dimensional neuropathological and biochemical data from brain banks. The goal is to computationally infer the most likely directed relationships between pathologies, formally testing if copathologies retain predictive power for clinical outcomes after conditioning on the stage and severity of primary AD tau and amyloid pathology, thereby revealing their true causal or reactive status.

ideas.title: Challenge linear progression of biological stages

ideas.title: A Multi-Modal, Contextual Reference Standard for AD Pathology
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that CSF biomarkers are the definitive gold standard for AD pathology by drawing an analogy from oncology. In cancer diagnostics, the field moved beyond relying solely on invasive tissue biopsies as the gold standard, recognizing tumor heterogeneity and sampling error. The analogous solution was the establishment of a multi-modal reference standard that synthesizes data from imaging, liquid biopsies, histopathology, and clinical presentation. Adapting this, the research proposes to develop and validate a new, composite reference standard for AD. This standard would contextually integrate CSF data with plasma biomarkers, tau-PET/amyloid-PET imaging, and other modalities using a defined algorithm. The goal is to create a more robust, representative, and clinically useful benchmark that reflects the full complexity of AD pathology, moving beyond the limitations of any single biofluid source.

ideas.description: This research project aims to establish the precise analytical performance specifications required for a blood-based p-tau217 ratio assay to be successfully integrated into a national public health screening program. Instead of a direct, abstract comparison between mass spectrometry and immunoassays, the work will model real-world screening outcomes—considering pre-test probabilities, confirmatory testing logistics, and cost-effectiveness—to determine the minimum threshold of accuracy and precision a platform must meet. The core synergy is the creation of a validated benchmark that guides assay development toward population-scale utility, ensuring that any platform meeting this benchmark is inherently fit for purpose in a public health system, thereby de-risking large-scale implementation.

ideas.title: Dynamic Biomarker State-Space Modeling for Disease Staging
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from control systems engineering, specifically state-space estimation techniques like Kalman filtering, to the problem of longitudinal biomarker monitoring. In the analogous field, hidden system states are optimally estimated from noisy temporal sensor data using a model of the system's dynamics. Adapted here, a mathematical model of biomarker production and clearance kinetics is integrated with sequential plasma p-tau217 and APS2 measurements. This approach filters out assay noise and biological variability to estimate the underlying, unobservable neuropathological disease state, its rate of change, and to probabilistically forecast future trajectories, thereby correlating more accurately with disease progression and enabling dynamic staging.

ideas.title: Applying Phase Transition Models to Alzheimer's Pathology
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that Alzheimer's disease progresses through strictly linear, irreversible biological stages. Drawing an analogy from condensed matter physics, where the linear progression of material phases (e.g., crystalline to amorphous) was overturned by demonstrating reversibility and non-sequential state changes through manipulation of energy barriers, we propose to adapt this framework. The adapted idea is to model Alzheimer's pathology as a phase landscape with metastable states. By identifying the molecular equivalents of 'energy barriers' and 'external stimuli'—such as specific protein homeostasis networks, metabolic perturbations, or lipid bilayer properties—the research would experimentally test whether established pathological hallmarks (amyloid-beta plaques, neurofibrillary tangles) can be made to regress or change order, investigating the potential for non-linear or reversible disease stages in cellular and animal models.

ideas.title: Prospective Multimodal Phenotyping to Resolve Temporal Dynamics Between Incipient Symptoms and Sub-Threshold Biomarker Flux
ideas.description: This research establishes a prospective longitudinal cohort of pre-symptomatic individuals at high genetic risk for defined disorders to capture the earliest transition to disease. It employs frequent, scheduled deep phenotyping using both traditional and novel digital/fluid biomarkers alongside structured clinical assessments to map the precise temporal sequence of biological and clinical change. The core analytical framework uses within-subject change-point modeling to determine if subtle biomarker deviations are detectable concurrent with or prior to the first validated clinical signs, moving beyond binary abnormality thresholds. This design directly tests the hypothesis of symptom precedence by controlling for etiology and capturing the dynamic, sub-threshold phase of biomarker evolution.

ideas.title: Seismic Tomography for the Brain: Inferring Regional Pathology from Systemic Biomarkers
ideas.description: This research adapts the geophysical inverse problem of seismic tomography to estimate regional brain amyloid/tau pathology from plasma biomarker levels. In seismology, the internal 3D structure of the Earth is inferred from surface measurements of seismic waves. The analogous solution involves constructing a forward model of wave propagation and using inverse methods to match predicted to observed data. Here, we adapt this by treating the brain as a medium with spatially variable pathology 'sources' (protein aggregates) that contribute to a pooled systemic signal (plasma biomarkers). We will develop a biophysical forward model simulating biomarker flux from distinct brain regions into the blood. Using a dataset of co-registered plasma assays and neuroimaging (PET/MRI), we will apply inverse problem-solving techniques (e.g., constrained linear inversion, Bayesian inference) to estimate the regional brain burden that best explains a given individual's plasma biomarker profile, moving from a single systemic measurement to a spatially resolved pathological map.

ideas.title: Calibrating a Liquid Biopsy Against a Gold-Standard Imaging Reference, Inspired by Satellite Ground-Truthing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from remote sensing, where new satellite data is validated through synchronized ground-truthing campaigns. The analogous problem is validating a new, less invasive measurement tool (plasma biomarkers) against an emerging but more established spatial reference standard (next-gen tau-PET). The adapted research idea is to conduct a temporally locked, cohort study where blood sampling and tau-PET imaging are performed in close succession. This direct, point-by-point validation protocol will determine the precision, dynamic range, and clinical concordance of the blood-based 'sensor' against the detailed neuroimaging 'map,' accelerating the development of calibrated blood tests for tau pathology.

ideas.description: This research direction develops machine learning models, inspired by oncology's multi-factorial risk models, that ingest multimodal patient data (e.g., imaging, biomarkers, genetics) to output a continuous, personalized disease progression score. Crucially, the model architecture is designed to attribute components of this risk score and the resulting stage discordance to specific, known copathologies. This moves beyond assessing correlation to provide a quantitative, patient-specific estimate of how much each copathology is contributing to the clinical syndrome, enabling more precise prognosis and targeted therapeutic strategies.

The core innovation is to test if these stage-adjusted biomarker thresholds are superior to universal cutoffs specifically for enriching clinical trial cohorts with patients exhibiting a discordance between their aggressive biological profile (per biomarkers) and their earlier clinical stage. This creates a dual-purpose tool: a validated method for setting context-aware biomarker limits and a direct application of those limits to recruit a biologically enriched, earlier-stage population likely to have a distinct prognosis and therapeutic response.

ideas.title: Adapting ELISA-to-Lateral Flow Principles from Food Safety for p-tau217 Ratio Quantification
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from food safety monitoring, where quantitative lab-based ELISA tests for contaminants like mycotoxins have been successfully adapted into semi-quantitative, reader-based lateral flow assays for field use. The analogous solution involves designing a competitive or sandwich immunoassay on a strip that yields a signal intensity proportional to analyte concentration, which is then measured by a portable optical reader. Adapted to the neuroscience context, the research would develop a lateral flow assay that uses two capture lines (or a multiplexed detection system) for p-tau217 and total tau217 on a single strip. A purpose-built, point-of-care reader would optically quantify the signal from each line and automatically compute the p-tau217 ratio, translating a complex laboratory biomarker ratio into a rapid, accessible clinical tool.

ideas.title: Detect Clinical Precedence via High-Resolution Phenotyping and Network Analysis
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that biomarker abnormality must precede or match clinical symptom severity. Drawing an analogy from ecosystem ecology, where subtle functional disturbances precede changes in standard biomass or chemical biomarkers, the adapted idea proposes a solution. The research would employ high-resolution, multi-modal clinical phenotyping—using digital wearables, advanced imaging, and granular behavioral or cognitive tests—to capture the earliest, subtlest signs of patient impairment. This dense phenotypic data would be analyzed alongside traditional biomarkers using dynamic network models to identify critical early nodes of dysregulation and novel 'phenotypic biomarker' signatures. The goal is to detect and model the point where clinical function begins to diverge from biomarker levels, potentially enabling much earlier intervention.

ideas.title: Stratified Prospective Validation of the APS2 Blood Test with Powered Subgroup Performance Analysis
ideas.description: This study employs a prospective, multi-center master protocol to validate the APS2 blood test using a pre-specified, stratified recruitment strategy that ensures adequate power for subgroup analyses. Cohorts are actively enriched to include balanced representation across key genetic ancestry groups, major comorbid disease states, and common medication regimens that could confound the test's biomarkers. The primary analysis mandates the independent calculation and reporting of all test performance metrics—including AUC, sensitivity, and specificity—for each demographic, clinical, and technical stratum. The final validation report will explicitly state whether the test meets pre-defined performance benchmarks in each subgroup, providing a transparent assessment of its generalizability and identifying any populations for whom the test is not recommended.

ideas.title: A Causal Inference Framework for Prospective Identification of Modifiers of Biological-Clinical Stage Discordance
ideas.description: This study establishes a prospective, deeply-phenotyped cohort of patients at initial clinical diagnosis to longitudinally track the divergence between repeated biological staging (e.g., via omics or imaging biomarkers) and clinical progression. It employs a causal inference framework, using Mendelian Randomization for genetic variants and carefully timed, repeated measures of lifestyle and environmental factors to distinguish causal modifiers from confounders. The design includes nested intervention trials on identified modifiable factors to test their direct impact on re-synchronizing biological and clinical stages, moving beyond association to actionable mechanistic and therapeutic insights.

ideas.title: Inter-batch variability as a confounder in detecting the clinical-biofluid disconnect
ideas.description: This research direction investigates how inter-batch analytical variability in biomarker assays can systematically obscure or mimic the clinical-biofluid disconnect. By mandating single-batch re-analysis of serial samples from patients who presented with clear clinical impairment but initially 'normal' biomarker levels, the study can distinguish true early clinical presentations from measurement drift. This approach controls for a major technical confounder, allowing for a more accurate identification of genuine prodromal cases where pathology is clinically manifest but not yet fully captured in biofluid. The outcome is a validated, methodologically stringent cohort of disconnect cases, crucial for discovering novel early disease mechanisms or more sensitive biomarker signatures.

ideas.title: Adapt ecological population dynamics modeling for biomarker kinetics
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution strategies from ecological population dynamics modeling to the problem of understanding biomarker temporal dynamics. In ecology, structured population models are used to understand how a heterogeneous population (with individuals in different states like age or size class) changes over time, influenced by environmental factors. The analogous solution involves modeling the flow and distribution of individuals across defined states. Adapted to pharmacokinetics, we model a population of biomarker molecules, not patients. The biomarker molecules are tracked through conceptual 'life stages' or physiological compartments (e.g., synthesis/release, central circulation, peripheral distribution, clearance). A population modeling approach is then used to estimate the rates of transition between these biomarker states from sparse, pooled clinical data. This allows the quantification of how patient-specific covariates (demographics, genetics, organ function) influence the fundamental kinetic processes governing biomarker appearance and disappearance in plasma, providing a mechanistic framework for interpreting longitudinal biomarker data in disease progression or therapeutic response.

ideas.title: Apply ecological community assembly frameworks to multi-omics AD subtyping
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Community Ecology to the problem of Alzheimer's disease (AD) subtyping via multi-omics integration. In ecology, the 'community assembly' framework is used to integrate data on species composition, environmental filters, and dispersal to classify communities (e.g., as niche-based or neutral) and predict their dynamics. The analogous solution is adapted by treating an individual's plasma proteomic profile as a 'molecular community,' where proteins are 'species,' genomic background acts as a heritable 'environmental filter,' and other factors (e.g., age, disease stage) are additional covariates. By applying ecological assembly models (e.g., stochastic vs. deterministic) to integrated plasma proteomics and genomics data, we can define novel AD subtypes based on the inferred biological 'assembly rules' driving an individual's molecular phenotype, moving beyond correlative clusters to mechanism-informed stratification.

ideas.title: Adapting Photolithography's Hybrid Approach to Immunoassay Development for p-tau217
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that mass spectrometry is the only viable platform for precise p-tau217 ratio quantification by drawing an analogy from semiconductor photolithography. In that field, the physical limit of optical wavelength was overcome not by a single tool, but through a suite of resolution enhancement techniques (RETs) like optical proximity correction, phase-shift masks, and multiple patterning—a hybrid, system-level engineering solution. Adapted to this context, the research idea is to develop a hybrid immunoassay system designed to collectively match MS performance. This would involve concurrently engineering: ultra-high-affinity antibody reagents, multi-stage signal processing and background reduction protocols, and machine learning-based calibration algorithms that use MS-derived data to correct for systematic assay biases. The core hypothesis is that the synergistic integration of these enhancements, mirroring the photolithography approach, can elevate a ligand-binding assay platform to the precision and accuracy level currently dominated by MS.

ideas.title: Enriching Clinical Trials Using Ecological Resilience Theory to Quantify Stage Discordance
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from ecology, where the discordance between an ecosystem's potential and realized state (ecological resilience) is used to predict recovery and target interventions. The analogous problem in clinical trials is the discordance between biological and clinical disease staging. We propose to develop a quantitative Biological-Clinical Discordance Index (BCDI) by integrating multi-omics data with clinical staging criteria. This index can then be used to stratify or enrich participant cohorts for clinical trials, specifically targeting patients with high discordance. The hypothesis is that these 'high-BCDI' patients, like highly disturbed ecosystems, may have fundamentally different progression risks and therapeutic response pathways, thereby increasing a trial's statistical power to detect treatment effects in biologically defined, high-need subgroups.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Probabilistic Modeling for Individualized Biomarker Interpretation
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to replace static biomarker cutoffs with dynamic, individualized interpretation models. It integrates a wide array of patient-specific covariates—including age, disease stage, sex, genetics, and comorbidities—into Bayesian models trained on diverse, multi-source clinical data. The system outputs patient-tailored probability distributions and likelihood ratios for clinical outcomes rather than a binary abnormal/normal classification. This approach directly addresses the multidimensional complexity of biomarker biology and provides clinicians with a more nuanced, evidence-based tool for decision-making.

ideas.title: Prospective Interventional Cohort for Causal Inference in Stage-Discordant Disease Trajectories
ideas.description: This study establishes a prospective, protocol-driven cohort of individuals with verified biological-clinical stage discordance, enrolling them at the point of discordance detection. All participants follow a standardized assessment and management pathway, with deep phenotyping via serial multi-omic, advanced imaging, and digital health monitoring at fixed intervals. The design explicitly measures and adjusts for potential confounders (e.g., treatment adherence, comorbidities, socioeconomic factors) to isolate the causal effect of the discordant state on hard clinical outcomes. This approach transforms passive tracking into an active causal inference engine, determining whether stage discordance is a driver of unique progression biology or a passive epiphenomenon.

ideas.title: Adapting Materials Science Composite Modeling to Identify Modifiers of Disease Staging
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from materials science, where micromechanical modeling identifies variables that explain discrepancies between a composite's predicted and actual performance. The analogous problem in medicine is the discrepancy between biological disease stage (from biomarkers, imaging, or molecular profiling) and clinical disease stage (symptoms, functional impairment). The adapted idea is to treat this discrepancy as a quantifiable 'performance gap.' Using a framework inspired by damage mechanics and statistical micromechanics, the study would design experiments and analyses to test specific genetic and lifestyle variables as potential modifiers. By applying integrative multi-omics, deep phenotyping, and advanced statistical modeling (e.g., mediation analysis, machine learning on high-dimensional data), the research aims to rank and quantify how each modifier explains the variance between biological and clinical staging, ultimately revealing key levers that alter disease trajectory.

ideas.title: Validate and Refine Digital Staging Biomarkers in Diverse Populations Using Computational Liquid Biopsy Analysis
ideas.description: This research fuses the validation of revised staging criteria with computational pathology for liquid biopsies to create a novel framework for equitable disease stratification. We will apply deep learning-based image analysis to quantify cellular and subcellular biomarkers from blood-based liquid biopsy samples (e.g., circulating tumor cells, extracellular vesicles) across demographically and geographically diverse cohorts. The resulting high-throughput, objective biomarker data will be used to rigorously test the performance and bias of existing biological staging criteria. This approach allows for the continuous refinement of staging models, ensuring they are robust, generalizable, and less susceptible to disparities arising from under-representation in training data.

Adapted to this clinical context, the research would involve a prospective, multi-center study where the APS2 test is run concurrently with the current diagnostic gold standard across several clinical sites. ideas.title: Iterative, Discovery-Driven Revision of Staging Criteria Using Diverse Cohorts
ideas.description: This research establishes a dynamic, two-phase system to move beyond simple validation of existing staging criteria. Phase 1 involves a global consortium study to rigorously test current criteria for equitable performance across demographic and geographic groups, explicitly mapping where and why they fail. Phase 2 initiates deep-phenotyping discovery studies within identified disparity cohorts to uncover novel biological, environmental, or social determinants of disease progression. The system culminates in a formal consensus and validation cycle to integrate these discoveries into revised, actionable staging criteria that are fundamentally more generalizable and precise.

ideas.title: Adapting Geochronological Standardization to Mitigate Diagnostic Batch Drift
ideas.description: The original idea challenges the assumption that single-batch analysis is sufficient for long-term clinical consistency. Drawing an analogy from geochronology, where the use of standard reference materials corrects for inter-laboratory and temporal variability in radiometric dating, this research proposes a novel solution. We adapt this by developing a stable, multi-analyte 'clinical chronometer' reference panel to be run in every batch of a given diagnostic test. By tracking the measured values of this panel over time, we can model and mathematically correct for batch-to-batch drift caused by changes in reagents, calibrators, and instrument performance. This approach moves beyond merely identifying variability to actively correcting for it, ensuring that a patient's result from 2025 is directly comparable to one from 2030, thereby safeguarding the longitudinal integrity of clinical diagnostics and biomarker studies.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Tracking of Stage-Discordant Individuals Using Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from ecology to study stage-discordant individuals. In ecology, scientists longitudinally track species whose actual habitat (realized niche) differs from their potential habitat (fundamental niche) to understand population dynamics. The analogous solution involves long-term monitoring, mark-recapture methods, and environmental modeling to predict trajectories. Adapted to medicine, we propose treating an individual's biological stage (from biomarkers, genomics, etc.) as their 'fundamental disease niche' and their clinical stage as the 'realized niche'. A longitudinal cohort study would track these discordant individuals with repeated, multi-modal assessments, akin to ecological monitoring. The goal is to model their unique progression trajectories, identify the internal (e.g., immune response) and external (e.g., treatment, lifestyle) factors that cause these niches to converge or diverge, and ultimately predict clinical outcomes and optimal intervention points.

ideas.title: Validate staging criteria using a global reference panel approach

The analogous solution involved aggregating data from multiple, globally sampled populations into a shared resource to ensure broad applicability. Adapted to the clinical research context, the idea is to form a consortium that builds a 'Global Clinical Staging Reference Panel' by harmonizing data from existing diverse cohorts worldwide. This panel would provide a standardized, multi-population dataset to rigorously test the revised biological and clinical staging criteria, validating their performance, calibration, and prognostic accuracy across a wide spectrum of demographic and geographic backgrounds before widespread clinical implementation.

ideas.title: Adapting Dynamic Reference Ranges from Forestry to Biomarker Cutoffs
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that static biomarker cutoffs are universally valid by drawing an analogy from forestry, where static Site Index tables were replaced by dynamic, climate-sensitive models. The analogous solution involved creating models that adjust the reference standard based on local environmental conditions. Adapted to biomedical research, the idea is to move beyond simply testing for necessary adjustments and instead build a predictive modeling framework. This framework would ingest key patient covariates (age, disease stage, sex, etc.) to generate a personalized, dynamic reference range or expected value for a biomarker, fundamentally shifting the diagnostic paradigm from a universal cutoff to a patient-contextualized benchmark.

ideas.title: Interpretable, Decomposable Dynamic Risk Scores with Real-Time Clinical Explanation
ideas.description: This system replaces black-box ML with an inherently interpretable model to generate personalized, continuous risk scores. It provides a real-time, interactive visualization that breaks down the score into the specific contribution of each input variable, such as a recent lab value or symptom report. This allows clinicians to immediately see which factors are elevating or mitigating risk, facilitating targeted clinical decisions and transparent patient conversations. The approach maintains the benefits of dynamic personalization while ensuring the model's reasoning is auditable and clinically actionable.

ideas.title: Adapt Seismic Signal Processing for Circulating Tumor Cell Detection in Liquid Biopsies
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from geophysics and seismology to the challenge of quantifying sparse biomarkers in blood-based liquid biopsies via digital pathology. The analogous problem in seismology involves extracting weak, meaningful reflected signals (representing subsurface oil reservoirs or geological strata) from overwhelming background noise in seismic data. That field's solution employs sophisticated signal processing techniques like wavelet transforms for multi-resolution analysis and deconvolution algorithms to sharpen signals. Adapted to computational pathology, these methods would be used to pre-process and analyze whole-slide images of liquid biopsy samples (e.g., blood smears or cytospins prepared from blood draws). The goal is to significantly enhance the detection, isolation, and morphological quantification of rare events like circulating tumor cells or clusters, treating them as distinct 'signatures' within the complex cellular background, thereby improving the sensitivity and analytical rigor of liquid biopsy-based diagnostics and monitoring.

ideas.title: High-content image analysis of cellular morphology in liquid biopsy cytology preparations
ideas.description: This research implements a computational pathology pipeline specifically designed for the cellular fraction of liquid biopsies. The system prepares peripheral blood mononuclear cell (PBMC) fractions or enriched circulating tumor cells (CTCs) onto standardized slides for high-resolution, automated microscopy. Deep learning models are trained to identify, classify, and morphologically characterize rare atypical cells against a background of normal blood cells. The output provides quantitative, spatially-resolved biomarkers—such as cell cluster geometry, nuclear features, and single-cell phenotypes—directly from liquid biopsy cytology, enabling a novel link between liquid biopsy sampling and tissue-like morphological analysis.
