ideas.title: Adaptive, Value-of-Information Based Phased Rollout for National Screening
ideas.description: This redesigned approach rejects a monolithic national launch in favor of a dynamic, learning system. It initiates screening within a defined, representative sentinel population network to gather real-world data on test performance, follow-up resource utilization, and patient outcomes. An integrated analytics engine continuously calculates the value of information and system burden, using these metrics to algorithmically adjust protocol parameters—such as risk-based eligibility criteria or reflex testing sequences—in near real-time. The system only triggers expansion to new populations once it empirically demonstrates operational stability, clinical utility, and cost-effectiveness within the current phase, ensuring the screening program is evidence-adaptive and resilient to performance drift.

ideas.title: Model the Full Cascade-of-Care to Determine Net System Economic Impact
ideas.description: This study evaluates the true cost-effectiveness of implementing a new blood test in primary care by modeling its complete downstream consequences, or 'cascade of care.' Using a combination of process mining on real-world clinical data and stakeholder interviews, it maps all subsequent diagnostic, treatment, and monitoring activities triggered by the test result, including unintended pathways. A system dynamics model then integrates these cascades to quantify the net impact on system-wide resource use and patient outcomes, providing a robust economic assessment that accounts for real-world clinical behavior beyond the idealized care pathway.

ideas.title: Adapting Environmental Sensor Network Architecture for National Biomarker Screening
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution architecture from large-scale environmental sensor networks to the challenge of scaling a blood test for national public health screening. In environmental science, monitoring air or water quality across a nation is achieved not by deploying identical, expensive instruments everywhere, but through a tiered system: a calibrated backbone of reference stations validates data from a widespread network of simpler, lower-cost sensors. We adapt this by designing a national screening strategy where a limited number of advanced regional public health laboratories act as the reference backbone, performing gold-standard assays and ensuring quality control. They would calibrate and validate the results flowing from a dense, decentralized network of simplified blood test points (e.g., in clinics, pharmacies, or mobile units). All data would feed into a central national public health analytics platform. This platform would employ computational models—analogous to those correcting for sensor drift or local interference in environmental networks—to harmonize data, account for inter-assay and inter-site variability, and produce a continuously updated, population-level dashboard of disease biomarkers. This architecture balances scalability, cost-effectiveness, and data integrity, enabling true population-level integration into public health systems.

ideas.title: Apply adaptive, personalized risk scoring from structural engineering to oncology thresholds
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) in engineering to the problem of static thresholds in oncology. In SHM, ML models analyze continuous sensor data from individual structures (like bridges) to create dynamic, personalized 'fatigue life' scores, replacing generic safety thresholds. The adapted research idea applies this by treating a patient as a unique biological structure. An ML model would be trained on a continuous stream of longitudinal, multi-modal patient data (labs, imaging, wearables, treatment history) to establish a personalized baseline and output a real-time, adaptive 'integrity score' for cancer risk. This creates a patient-specific, continuously updated risk assessment that reflects individual disease trajectory and treatment response, fundamentally replacing the one-size-fits-all fixed cutoff paradigm with a dynamic, personalized monitoring system.

ideas.title: Microfluidic Paper-Based Analytical Devices (μPADs) for Fingerstick Blood Collection
ideas.description: This research seed proposes scaling down blood collection to fingerstick volumes for at-home testing. Drawing an analogy from environmental science, where paper-based microfluidic devices solved the problem of performing multiplexed chemical analyses on tiny, single drops of water in resource-limited field settings, we adapt this solution to the biomedical context. The adapted idea is to develop a disposable, paper-based micro-sampling device for capillary blood. A single fingerstick drop is applied to the device, which uses capillary action through pre-defined hydrophilic channels and filtration zones to separate plasma and conduct simultaneous, low-cost colorimetric assays for multiple biomarkers, enabling comprehensive remote health monitoring without the need for venipuncture or complex instrumentation.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop Pre-Analytical Control for At-Home Micro-Sampling
ideas.description: This system combines a patient-friendly capillary blood sampler with integrated, on-device pre-analytical processing to eliminate user-induced errors. The disposable cartridge uses microfluidic gates and optical sensors to confirm a metered, sufficient volume of blood, then automatically mixes it with pre-loaded stabilizers to preserve target analytes. Data on collection time, volume, and quality is digitally tagged to the sample. This ensures that only specimens meeting strict pre-analytical criteria are analyzed, making remote testing as reliable as a phlebotomist-collected venous draw for a defined panel of biomarkers.
